About the Authors Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues. At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel: In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom 96 Drum Performance 4

Chapter 3: Basic Independence The study of coordinated independence is essential in order to gain command and control of the drumset: The method presented in this (and upcoming units) introduces you to a "tried and true" method to begin developing the control needed to perform at a high level on your instrument To acquire these skills, you must develop disciplined and meticulous practice method There is no substitute for practicing slowly and in a controlled manner In these exercises, the ride cymbal and hi-hat will play a continuous pattern while the snare drum and bass drum are placed in various downbeat and upbeat groupings The cymbal pattern is a common pattern used in all genres of music which is a quarter note on beats 1 and 3 and two eighth notes on beats 2 and 4. In addition, the hi-hat is played with the foot on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 1.9 Ride cymbal Hi-hat with foot When played as straight eighth notes this pattern can be used in Latin, funk; rock, R&B, metal, etc: This common pattern can also be interpreted as swung eighth notes for swung styles such as jazz and hip-hop: As presented in Drum Performance 3, swung eighth notes may be indicated at the top of a chart with a symbol indicating all written eighth notes are swung unless otherwise indicated. For example: Ex 1.10 J-r;_ S Swung eighth notes indicator Equals General Rules There are a few general rules to keep in mind when beginning independence exercises 1 . When the snare drum (or bass drum) lands on a downbeat; it will always be paired with the ride cymbal. Ride cymbal and snare drum together on every downbeat Ex 1.11 Drum Technique 3

I6th Note Conversion The sticking from the six variations from above (Ex. 5.1 and 5.2) can also be played over 16th notes This application with open up your ability to play creative fills in rock and funk feels. This also creates an asymmetrical grouping of three I6th notes Therefore; this pattern will go over the bar We'll start with variations stopping before the pattern goes over the bar as follows: Ex 5.3 Right handed 3,, L LR   R L LR 3  LR R L UR   R [ UR   R L UR  R E  ,) L LR R L LR R L UR R [ &R Ex 5.4 Left handed 7= F8F3S R   R ~R @ L  R   r L = 1 R   Rl L R   RL L R   RL R   R L R   RL R) L R  RL 48 Drum Technique 4

Unit 0 Chapter Unit SEVEN reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Basic Linear Concepts The definition of linear drumming is that no two (or more) voices sound at the same time: Linear drumming concepts are great ways to expand your technique and creativity. In fact;you most likely already have played linear ideas without even knowing it To display this concept in the simplest form; take a look at a simple rock beat for example: Ex 7.1 In the above example (Ex. 7.1) the hi-hat and bass drum play at the same time on beats and 3.On beats 2 and 4 the hi-hat and the snare drum play at the same time Ifyou remove the hi-hat from the downbeats of beats 1,2,3,and 4 (leaving the hi-hat playing solely on the upbeats), a simple linear groove is created, as follows: Ex 7.2 Asyou can see in the example above (Ex. 7.2), no two voices are sounding (playing) at the same time. This is a very simple example of a linear groove: What happens when linear grooves are played is the template of subdivision (normally coming from the hi- hat) no longer exists You do not have a steady stream of eighth notes present to help other voices"line up" with one another Therefore; your ability to mentally subdivide must be very strong: One great exercise to get you started is to play the following exercise, which exploits the"es"and "ahs" of each beat: I6th-Note Trainer Ex 7.3 60 Drum Technique 4

Chapter 2: Drum Soloing Concepts Drum solos are a chance for the drummer to shine; be creative and add excitement to a piece of music: When taking a drum solo it is very easy to get overwhelmed with ideas, therefore you must think ahead and have a seed of an idea to start your solo. You can then develop your idea in similar fashion to the concepts presented in this book (compression/expansion; over-the-bar ideas, etc ). One way to build your own repertoire of"go to"ideas is to research past drummers""go to"ideas. Drummers such as Philly Joe Jones, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams, John Bonham, Neil Peart and Steve Jordan have provided the drumming community with classic concepts that are essential to the development of great drum soloing: Drum solos can be categorized as follows: The "open" solo: An extended, unaccompanied drum solo that typically changes tempos and has rubato (free-form, out-of-time) sections Soloing over the form: Common in the jazz idiom, this style of solo follows the form (AABA, ABAB, Blues; etc) of the tune just as a melodic instrumentalist would: Soloing over a rhythmic vamp: A drum solo over a set of ensemble figures, usually in a two or four-bar pattern: Groove-based solo: This drum solo is primarily a groove with variations and fill ideas either unaccompanied or over a bass line: Examples of Each Solo Style Open Solos Open solos were very popular in the classic rock era of the 1970s and 1980s. Drummers such as John Bonham; lan Paice; Carl Palmer and Neil Peart are commonly associated with this style of soloing: Below are few brief highlights from this era. John Bonham uses nice phrasing in this 32nd-note lick featuring doubles on a single bass drum, as follows: Ex 10.10 Bonham then plays a triplet-based idea that was originally played by Elvin Jones For example: Ex 10.11 F E2 Neil Peart also pays homage to the early jazz masters by "borrowing" this idea from Art Blakey: Note: Billy Cobham got a lot of mileage out ofthis idea as well: Ex 10.12 88 Drum Technique 4

Unit 10 - Chapter 2 Groove-Based Solos Groove solos are great to use in rock and funk genres where the groove just cannot be interrupted by drumming calisthenics Drummers such as Steve Jordan use a minimalistic approach and usually limit their orchestration to just bass drum; snare drum and cymbals Ex 10.17 F3 33 233 4DA FEF 5 9

Jeff Bowders began his journey of drumming in 1987. Since then; he has been blessed with the opportunity to perform and/or teach in over 50 countries Whether it is backing artist such as Paul Gilbert; Puddle of Mudd, Shakira and host of  others, or performing as a solo artist at drum festivals and camps, Jeff's passion for drums and drumming is undeniable: An in-demand educator at Musicians Institute in Los Angeles since 2003, Jeff's critically acclaimed MI Press Hal Leonard Publications include Essential Rock Drumming Concepts book/CD; Double Bass Drumming-The  Mirrored Groove System book/CD; Essential Drumset Fills-The Component Rhythm System book/CD and Double BassDrumming Workshop DVD. Many of the concepts from Jeff's publications, including those in this book; can be heard on Jeff's solo album The Pilgrimage of Thingamuhjig: Jeff is honored to be endorsed by these amazing companies: Tama Drums and Hardware; Sabian Cymbals, Vater Drumsticks and Evans Drumheads: wwWieffbowderscom 93

Since moving to LA. in 2003, Kevin Stevens has played with the house band (Cleto and the Cletones) for "Jimmy Kimmel Live" performed with Shakira on The Ellen DeGeneres Show" toured the globe with the likes of Chilean pop superstar Beto Cuevas (La Ley), the critically acclaimed Neon Neon, and actress/singer Minnie Driver Kevin was a member of the original cast for the hit Broadway musical/movie Rock   of   Ages and is also the leader/founder of the internationally renowned funk/jazz combo Brass Monkey Brass Band. Kevin is graduate of the acclaimed University of Miami jazz department with a BA in Studio Music and Jazz Performance. Since arriving at Musicians Institute in 2009, Kevin has been known as one of the instructors to go to in order to get your groove together: Kevin teaches Private Lessons, Drumset Performance, Music Minus One,   along with New Orleans Drumming a class which he created for Ml which covers the history ofthe drum set and the role of the instrument in jazz, R&B, rock & roll, funk and beyondl Kevin is proud to endorse these fine companies: Sabian Cymbals, Vic Firth Drumsticks, Remo Drumheads and Protection Racket Cases: 94 Drum Technique 4

Unit twO reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Hard Rock "Rock 'n' roll"is often used as a generic term, but its sound is rarely predictable: From the outset; when the early rockers merged country and blues, its energy rebellion and catchy hooks have defined rock As a result; everything from Chuck Berry's pounding; three-chord rockers and the sweet harmonies of the Beatles to the jarring; atonal white noise of Sonic Youth has been categorized as "rock" Today, we will play a chart in the style of the Foo Fighters: Structure: Sixteen-Bar Phrase This chart employs a common structure; which is used in most rock and pop music: the sixteen-bar phrase: As its title suggests, this passage is sixteen bars (measures) long and each bar (after the first bar) is marked with either time slashes or single-bar repeats Furthermore, each sixteen-bar phrase can be augmented with material from last weeks lesson, such as rehearsal letters, repeats signs and additional eight-bar phrases a5 well: Ex 2.1 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents 7 7E ) 13 Single-Bar Repeats: single-bar repeat is a sign indicating that you must repeat a particular bar (or measure): Rather than marking beats (like time slashes), single-bar repeats mark measures (all four beats) There is usually one specific bar of notated music at the beginning of a phrase and then multiple uses of single bar repeats thereafter: For example: Ex 2.2 Medium Hi-Hat w/Quarter-Note Accents 13

Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Slow 12-Bar Blues The blues is one of America's greatest musical treasures. A roots music form that evolved out of African- American work songs, field hollers, spirituals,and country string ballads more than a century ago, the blues is the foundation of virtually every major American music form born in the ZOth century, including jazz, rhythm and blues, rock 'n'roll, and hip-hop. This unit's chart will focus on modern master of blues: Stevie Ray Vaughan: Structure: 12-Bar Blues (I-IV-V) The term "12-bar blues" refers to the number of measures, or musical bars, used to express the theme of a typical blues song: A 12-bar blues is divided into three four-bar segments A standard blues progression typically features three chords based on the first written as first (I), fourth (IV), and fifth notes ofan eight- note scale: In this case; the (B7) chord dominates the first four bars, the IV (E7) chord typically appears in the second four bars and the V (F#7) chord is played in the third four bars Ex 6.1 All B7 24 37 7 7 IV 3 3 E7 2044/ B7 V F#7 E7 B7 F#7 04 Although the 12-bar blues is broken up into four four-bar phrases that outline the |-IV-V chord structure; it is important to realize that you are ultimately dealing with a 12-bar phrase: Rehearsal Letters Just like the 32-bar AABA and ABAB are performed in 32-bar increments; this new structure will also get repeated in its phrase length of 12 bars: In this chart each set of 12-bars is labeled asan A section with each new 12-bars having a numeric suffix For example: Ex 6.2 Al A2 A3 A4 51

"12/8 Feel" within a 4/4 Time Signature: The definition of a triplet is three notes of the same value occupying the same space as two notes of the same value: Basically, each beat; instead of being divided by twos or fours, is divided by thirds Dividing each beat into three eighth note triplets creates 12 eighth notes within one measure of 4/4 time (3X4-12). Rather than writing four groups of three note sub-phrases within 12/8 time signature (6/8 and 12/8 will be addressed in Reading 2) , many composers prefer to write four groups of triplets within 4/4 time. This is called a"12/8 Feel" Maintaining the time signature of 4/4 allows for the downbeats to remain as 1, 2,3,and 4.This weeks drum chart is written in 4/4,with a tempo of a quarter note equaling 60 beats per minute: For example: Ex 6.3 4 60 Slow Blues Al Ride Cymbal 3 Counting Eighth-Note Triplets: There are a few methods to counting eighth-note triplet subdivisions. One great way is to simply use the word "triplet" preceded by the beat number: For example: Ex 6.4 3 3 3 3 trip Iet 2 trip let 3 trip let 4 trip let Multiple-Bar Repeat Signs: A multiple-bar repeat sign functions the same way asa single-bar repeat sign, except it instructs you to repeat a certain amount of previous bars. If you have a two-bar phrase that needs to be repeated, you can simply use a modified bar repeat sign with the exact amount of measures that need to be repeated placed above the symbol: For example: Ex 6.5 A2 Tight Hi-Hat 2-bar phrase Repeat the previous 2 bars 52 Drum Performance
Chapter 3: Slow 12-Bar Blues Play-Along Unit 6 "One" Ex 6.21 "Two" "Three "Four" 60 Slow Blues Al Ride Cymbal 5 A2 Tight Hi-Hat 2 13 3 17 21 3 3 7 23 58 Drum Performance

Unit Seven reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Shuffle 12-Bar Blues Todays chart will focus on a standard blues rhythm: the shuffle: Structure Il: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) Open Form: The 12-bar blues is considered an open form. This means that the song (or chart) is played (and subsequently repeated) in 12-bar increments without a specifically stated ending: Therefore; the 12-bar form (chart) will be performed in 12-bar increments (12-24-36-48-60-72 bars): With this fact in mind; there are two additional terms that modify, add to and accompany this form: They are: (1) the head and (2) a chorus Lets take a look at each in greater detail: The Head The term 'head" is most often used in jazz or blues-related contexts, and it refers to the thematic melody within a song's form. This melody is usually played at the beginning and at the end of a song's performance: Thus, if a bandleader requests one head (in a 12-bar form), that would mean that the band would play through 12 bars ofthe melody. For example: Ex.7.1 Melody Line Swing J = 120 F7 3 Bb7 F7 Bb7 F7 C7 € 3 Take Coda on cue Jazz and blues musicians also often give a signal (or cue) for the "head" or "top" by patting their hand on top of their head, which is meant to make sure everybody "goes back to the melody" the next time the "top of the form" comes around: 61

A Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus which refers to "the hook" of a song ~a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form" Thus,if a bandleader says they: want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12-bar blues form), that would be one 12-bar blues form of guitar solo. For example: Ex 7.2 Guitar Solo through the form IV IV V_ 10 Head and Chorus Terms Used Together Oftentimes a bandleader will state the form at the beginning ofa song He or she might say something like this: Were going to play one head, one chorus of guitar solo and one head out" Thus (in a 12 bar form), they want the band to play 12 bars ofthe melody; 12 bars (one time through the 12-bar form) of guitar solo and one 12-bar melody to the end. For example: Ex 7.3 Head (12 bars) One Chorus of Guitar Solo (12 bars) Head Out (12 bars) 62 Drum Performance

Unit 7 Chapter 1 Cue: As stated within the open form terminology, there is no specific ending within this form. Thus, a cue is employed. A cue is a notice made from one band member to the rest of the band in order to signal a break; rest or ending: Thus, in the chart you will see the text "on cue" This will tell you to get the band's attention by playing a short fill or counting them into the coda ending: Coda As definied in Unit 4,a coda is a specific ending; which is represented by this symbol (left). Therefore; after your cue; the band will take the coda sign after bar 8, and follow it down to the actual coda (marked by the same symbol) This is where the chart will end. For example: Ex 7.4 Bb7 A7 Ab7 3 G7 CzsuS4) C7 C#7 C7 7 Take Coda on cue F? Fi7   F7 5 Shuffle Feel: A shuffle feel is a triplet-based groove written in 4/4 time It is very common in blues songs, medium tempo jazz pieces and in rock music The shuffle usually refers to a "broken triplet feel;" where there is no middle triplet present: For example: Ex 7.5 3 It is also usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 7.6 3 Played as eighth note triplets This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as a "broken" eighth-note triplet: 63

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Review Sound: 1 . Any bass drum articulation:"bury the beater" or open articulation: 2 Rimshot snare sound: 3. Medium-even hi-hat sound. A different groove order and form for each student: Now that you understand the elements, will break down the grooves for each part of the 12-bar form performance: Assigning the grooves: Each student will be assigned a particular groove for the head and the choruses: Ex 7.7 Groove #I:"Four on the Floor Ex 7.8 Groove #2: "Syncopated" Assigning a form for each student: Each student will also be assigned particular number of heads and choruses of guitar solo for their performances a5 well: Marking the 12-bar form: hi-hat for the head and ride cymbal for the choruses: In the last few charts, we marked form by changing either the groove or riding surface for each rehearsal letter: However; the approach will differ slightly within a 12-bar-blues "open" form. As previously discussed, you will be repeating the same 12-bar phrase over and over: However; there will be two main sections within this song: the head and the chorus of solo. Thus,you will play thehi-hat during the head and the ride cymbal (with your hi-hat foot moving) during the guitar solo choruses Upon the completion ofthe guitar solo (and return to the head), you will return to the hi-hat: 64 Drum Performance

Borrowing the idea of an instrumental combo and loud amplification from rock'n'roll, the hybrid became quite popular and the bands that immediately followed (Cream; Led Zeppelin, ZZ Top) were louder and more riff-oriented With this in mind, this weeks chart is in the modern tradition of the John Mayer Trio and groove master Steve Jordan: Guitar Introduction Although the drum groove is written in bars and 2 ofthe chart; this week's song features a four-bar guitar introduction: This is stated by the text: Ist Time: Guitar Only. Unit 3 - Chapter 1 12/8 Another very common eighth note-based time signature is 12/8. In modern music; 12/8 is the basis for many slow blues and pop ballad tunes In 12/8 there are twelve eighth notes in every measure; with each eighth note being a downbeat: For example: Ex 3.9 2 3 4 5 6 8 10 11 12 Similar to 6/8,ifthe tempo is fast 12/8 can be felt in "four" with the dotted quarter note receiving the strong beats on beats 1,4,7 and 10 as follows: Ex 3.10 8 10 11 12 12 35

Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Jazz Jazz was created at the beginning ofthe ZOth century by blending rich African rhythms with sophisticated European harmony (chord structure): Improvisation and group interaction are two of the key elements that make jazz a great vehicle for creative drumming: This unit's chart will focus on a jazz interpretation of the blues, which allows us to focus on the jazz ride cymbal pattern, kick and snare comping, and a 12-bar drum solo Structure: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 6,the most common musical form of blues is the 12-bar blues. In this case (because we are in the key of G), the (G7) chord spans the first four bars; the IV (C7) chord typically appears in the second set of four bars and the V (D7) chord is played in the third set of four bars. For example: Ex 6.1 G 1 D GI D. C al Coda Jazz Terminology Review As mentioned in Performance 1,_ Unit 7,there are a few key - terms and concepts related to jazz and blues music that are essential to understand. Open Form The term open form means that the song is played (and repeated) in 12-bar increments, without a specifically stated ending: Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus, which refers to "the hook" of a song,a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form:" If a bandleader says they want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12- bar blues form) , that would be mean one pass through the 12-bar blues form; but with a guitar solo (in place of the melody). Cue Jazz and blues musicians often give each other the "head" or"top" cue by patting their hand on top of their head,which is usually meant to make sure everybody goes back to the melody the next time the top e ofthe form comes around; 52 Drum Performance 2

Unit 6 - Chapter 1 The Head The term head refers to the thematic melody within a song's form. The head for this chart contains many syncopated and recurring swung-eighth-note rhythms For example: Ex 6.2 G? 24 4EEEN G7 "E8 34} D7 G7 D. C al Coda Swing Eighth Notes As mentioned in Performance 1, Units 6 and 7, jazz and blues are usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied. This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 6.3 =JeJ This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as & "broken" eighth-note triplet: This. applies to the notated drum solo as well: 53

Unit 6 - Chapter 2 Comping the Choruses of Guitar Solo During the choruses of solo, the snare drum and bass drum accompany and react to the soloist This is not the time to display complicated jazz independence exercises underneath your ride cymbal and hi-hat patterns: However; independence is needed to properly execute many of the most tasteful and musical phrases. Therefore; listed below are 12 examples of basic comping patterns for your review: Play the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4.These patterns can be played on both the snare drum and bass drum as well or you can mix them up between the bass drum and snare drum: Comping Examples Ex 6.13 Jazz Drumming: Marking a Song's Form A drummer's most important job is to play a great groove with steady time However; these attributes are not enough to make music. We have another very important duty to perform: marking a songs form. Marking form is outlining (and drawing attention to) each different section within a song: Here; these sections are the head and chorus ofthe 12-bar blues This is accomplished by employing sonic changes to the drumset by changing ride cymbals for each new section of the song: (In jazz, all cymbals are considered rides ) The head should have a particular cymbal/texture and the choruses of solo should have different cymbal/texture: In today's chart; the following form, performance instructions and cymbal will be used: Ex 6.14 1_ Head (ride cymbal 1) Comping the head (melody) Ride Cymbal #1 2 Chorus of guitar solo (ride cymbal 2) Ride Cymbal #2 3. Chorus 2 of guitar solo Comping with the soloist and feathering the bass drum 4 Chorus of drum solo (You will prepare this for the second class of the week:) One 12-bar drum solo with stick-shot accents in bars 5-8 5_ Head ~taking the coda (ride cymbal 1) Ride Cymbal #1 57

Unit SevEN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Bossa Nova Bossa nova is a style of Brazilian music popularized by Antonio Carlos Jobim, Vinicius de Moraes and Joao Gilberto. The bossa nova movement (which is Portuguese for "new trend") lasted six years (1958-63),and it contributed a number of songs to the standard jazz repertoire Today's chart will focus on a classic bossa nova drumming method, which is characterized by continuous eighths on the hi-hat (or ride cymbal), a typical two-bar cross-stick pattern and the bass drum playing eighth notes on "1-83-&1" Ex 7.1 Bossa Nova Notice that this is a two-bar cross-stick phrase The first bar has the pattern being played on beat 1, the"&" of 2 and beat 4.The second bar has the cross-stick pattern on beats 2 and the"&"of 3.It is also important to keep the bass drum pattern consistent; without any rhythmic variations AAB Open Form The form of this chart is AAB with each section being eight bars in length: This form is open (similar to the open 12-bar blues form), which means the AAB (24-bar) form circulates after the head (melody over AAB the first time) for solo choruses. With these facts in mind; the normal usages of the head and chorus (from Unit 6) still apply: Head = melody Chorus one time through the form Form Addition: Four-Bar Intro Vamp Unit 7's chart has a four-bar introduction that augments the AAB form: During the introduction, the drums continue to play the bossa nova ostintato while opening the hi-hat on the "&"of beat 4 and on beat 1_ Ex 7.2 The phrase "vamp until cue"is used to instruct you to continue to play the four-bar intro until you receive a visual cue (usually a confirming head nod). Since the guitar plays the melody at A,the guitar player will be in charge of giving the cue You must make sure you are keeping an eye on the guitar player during the intro. The four-bar intro may be played for varying lengths of time the song is performed, but the duration will always be a multiple of four (8, 12, 16,20,etc ). Ex 7.3 Bossa _ 108 Vamp_ until cue After Cue 5 62 Drum Performance 2

Unit 4 - Chapter 1 Phrasing, Target Beats and Note Lengths Just like straight eighth-note rock feels; triplet-based charts also follow the same rules regarding phrasing; target beats and note length. However; the feel and note placement change: Let's take a lookat an example from Unit with a swing feel instead of a rock feel: Ex 4.8 Figure 7 In a big band or blues style chart; the above figure (Ex. 4.8) may me voiced with very clear set-up fills and accented figure crashes For example: Ex 4.9 Figure We can also incorporate full triplets For example: Ex 4.10 Figure 3 > 3> Notice that the triplet fill (on beat 4 of the third measure) has accents on the first and last eighth notes These accents help to shape to fill and make it stylistically correct Ex 4.11 45

Unit five Unit 5 - Chapter 3 reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts In this unit; two-bar swung eighth note ensemble figures will be presented within a practical setting These exercises will help you develop the ability to interpret ensemble figures as they relate to the modern music world. In addition, by using triplet fill-ins and snare drum rolls, we will also lookat other ways to orchestrate fills (and figures): Triplet Fill-Ins As you learned in Reading 2, it is essential to be able to read an eighth-note triplet-based figure, while also filling in consecutive triplets (i.e: either alternating or right-hand lead) . Placing triplets between ensemble figures is a great way to maintain your time feel and keep - the momentum moving forward within a chart: Let's take a look at applying right-hand lead and hand-to-hand triplets: Ex 5.1 Figure This "right-hand lead" example would work well in a jazz combo setting: R R This "hand-to-hand" example would work well in a rock or blues setting: R R L   R    R R Depending on the style of music; the example above (or a hybrid-combination ofthe two) will helpyou with ensemble figure beat placement and set-up fill development: 52 Drum Reading 3

Unit 5 Chapter 3 Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Slow Swing Grooves and Subdividing Swing blues is a jazz-tinged style of blues that came to prominence in the mid-to-late 1940s. Usually featuring a vocalist in front of a large; horn-driven orchestra, the style is earmarked by intensely shouted vocals,honking tenor saxophone solos and a driving rhythm section. The lyrics are celebratory in nature,and the music was the bridge between the older styles of small band blues and the big band jazz sound of the 1940s. Structure: 12-Bar Blues Form This 12-bar blues form is comprised of three different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter: A, A2, A3. The head (and main riffs) appears within A and A2, and A3 is a 12-bar chorus of guitar solo. For example: Ex 6.1 60 Slow Swing ### Letter A: First chorus with breaks: SlA2| Letter A2: Second chorus with breaks: Also fourth chorus due to D.S. A3 Letter A3: Third chorus for guitar solo. D. Chapter 2: Drum Soloing Concepts Drum solos are a chance for the drummer to shine; be creative and add excitement to a piece of music: When taking a drum solo it is very easy to get overwhelmed with ideas, therefore you must think ahead and have a seed of an idea to start your solo. You can then develop your idea in similar fashion to the concepts presented in this book (compression/expansion; over-the-bar ideas, etc ). One way to build your own repertoire of"go to"ideas is to research past drummers""go to"ideas. Drummers such as Philly Joe Jones, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams, John Bonham, Neil Peart and Steve Jordan have provided the drumming community with classic concepts that are essential to the development of great drum soloing: Drum solos can be categorized as follows: The "open" solo: An extended, unaccompanied drum solo that typically changes tempos and has rubato (free-form, out-of-time) sections Soloing over the form: Common in the jazz idiom, this style of solo follows the form (AABA, ABAB, Blues; etc) of the tune just as a melodic instrumentalist would: Soloing over a rhythmic vamp: A drum solo over a set of ensemble figures, usually in a two or four-bar pattern: Groove-based solo: This drum solo is primarily a groove with variations and fill ideas either unaccompanied or over a bass line: Examples of Each Solo Style Open Solos Open solos were very popular in the classic rock era of the 1970s and 1980s. Drummers such as John Bonham; lan Paice; Carl Palmer and Neil Peart are commonly associated with this style of soloing: Below are few brief highlights from this era. John Bonham uses nice phrasing in this 32nd-note lick featuring doubles on a single bass drum, as follows: Ex 10.10 Bonham then plays a triplet-based idea that was originally played by Elvin Jones For example: Ex 10.11 F E2 Neil Peart also pays homage to the early jazz masters by "borrowing" this idea from Art Blakey: Note: Billy Cobham got a lot of mileage out ofthis idea as well: Ex 10.12 88 Drum Technique 4

CD Track 1Let's Play One On this track we have decided to start with brushes, as it seems to fit the mood and the tempo so well. You could also try playing the tune with sticks. Entire books have been written about playing with brushes, but here is some basic information to get you going: Motion, as with the ride cymbal, is key to playing a swinging, relaxed time feel Practice playing a smooth lega- to sweep very lightly on the drum head with no pulse or accent: Next, add quarter notes with the right hand: Be sure the time is fluid sounding: Here is a basic pattern that will work with this tune: RH Start Here 2 4 1,a,3,a LH Start Here Here are four points to focus on: 1. Relax and work on a fluid graceful motion with the brush_ 2 . Keep your motion more horizontal than vertical_ 3. Feel the triplet subdivision under the quarter-note pulse 4. Let the brush rest lightly against the head, and avoid pushing it into the head except for effects: It is very important for the drummer to know exactly where helshe is in the musical form, otherwise the drum- ming can sound random and unmusical. The drummer'$ playing should always outline and support the form_ One simple way to outline the structure is to play a small fill at the end of the form. In this tune; the form is 12 bars long, SO try playing a light fill at the end of bar 12. Use some of the turnaround figures from the previous section. During the bass solo, you might want to change textures by playing the ride pattern on the closed hi-hat with a brush: You also might try riding on a cymbal with a brush. During the drum trades, realize that the 12-bar blues form continues while you are playing the trades: For this song, we have provided you with a lead sheet, a full drum chart, and an abbreviated drum chart: On a real gig, you might see any of these three options, or no chart at all: It is important for the drummer to be able to understand the form of the song and perform it accurately from any of these different charts_ The full drum chart will only be provided for the first two songs in this book, after which the lead sheet and abbreviated drum chart will be provided_

CD Track 2  MmmPick It Up A Little This is a nice 12-bar blues at a medium swinging tempo. This is a perfect tempo to practice some of the comp- ing exercises from the book, and also work on feathering the bass drum: Try playing quarter notes on the ride cymbal with your eyes closed to really feel the pulse with the bass player: Are your quarter notes even, solid, and balanced? Are you comfortable and balanced on the set? Is the bass drum feathered lightly and controlled? Is there good balance between the bass drum, hi-hat, and ride cymbal? Try to outline the form of the 12-bar blues. Play a turnaround figure to mark the top of each new 12-bar section. During the bass solo, try playing time on the hi-hat: Realize that you don't want to totally close the cymbals, but leave them slightly open to avoid a "vertical" time feel. The great masters often used the left thumb and first finger to slightly close the cymbals to create more legato feel. During the four-bar solo trades (trading fours) , understand that the form keeps moving, so that on the first cho- rus Of "4s, you would play the second four bars of the 12-bar tune_ On the second chorus, you will play the first and third sets of four bars. This tune has a classic ending, so memorize it! "MMMpick It Up A Little" Leao Sheet J =Jlo F7 877 F7 5}+ 861 867 51 Gsus7/8 867 07 4-7 @7

Unit three reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Sixteenth Note Notation and Values The I6th note is another essential rhythmic value: Most funk; fusion and metal styles employ this essential subdivision: A I6th note looks like a quarter note but with two flags attached: For example: Ex 3.1 Stem Flags Note head Sixteenth notes are equal to one 16th of a measure in 4/4.By dividing a quarter note (equal to one beat) into four equal parts, the byproduct is now four I6th notes There are four quarter notes in one measure and therefore sixteen I6th notes in one measure (4x4=16). You can also think of 16th notes as an eighth note divided into two equal parts Thus, one-quarter note (1/4) is equal to two eighth notes (2/8), which are also equal to four I6th notes (4/16) all of which are equal to one beat in 4/4 time (i.e. 1/4-2/8-4/16). For example: Ex 3.2 Note Value Breakdown One Quarter 1/4 Two Eighths 2/8 Four Sixteenths 4/16 Further breakdown One Quarter Note = Two Eighth Notes 1/4 = 2/8 One Eighth Note = Two Sixteenth Notes 1/8 = 2/16 An One Quarter Note = Four Sixteenth Notes 1/4 = 4/16 Two Eighth Notes Four Sixteenth Notes 2/8 = 4/16 51

Chapter 3: Sixteenth Triplets & Swung 16ths Sixteenth-note triplets are the result of dividing an eighth-note triplet in two. In 4/4 time there are six 16th- note triplets for every beat (notated as a group of six with the number "6" above the double beam [false notation]) Sixteenth-note triplets are counted as"1-trip-let-&-trip-let-2-trip-let-&-trip-let-3-trip-let-&-trip-let- 4-trip-let-&-trip-let" For example: Ex 4.9 3 trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let & trip let trip let & trip let trip let & trip let trip let & trip let In Reading 1, Unit 6 you learned about swung eighth-note notation: In this unit you will learn about swung 16th-note notation. Swung I6th notes are widely used in modern rock; funk and hip-hop. To economize notation; the rhythms in these genres are normally written as regular I6th notes to avoid monotonous usage of rests and brackets: Swung I6th notes are based on 16th-note triplets. Similar to the "broken eighth-note triplet" needed to create a shuffle feel,a swung I6th-note feel employs a "broken I6th-note triplet" To create broken I6th-note triplets; we remove the first and fifth note. In the example below, you will notice that the proper I6th triplet counting is notated in the top staffwith the swung 16th-note counting applied directly beneath it: In addition; full 16th-note triplets are notated on the bottom staff for your reference: For example: Ex 4.10 Sixteenth-note triplets 3 trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let Counting four beatsapertmeasure in4/4 "Broken" 16th-note triplets 52 Drum Reading 2

Unit five reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: R&B: Motown and Stax During the 1960s and 70s, Motown and Stax were prominent and successful R&B record labels Because of their huge radio and sales success, these label names have actually become sub-genres of R&B music in their own right: Many successful artists of today such as Adele; Bruno Mars, _ Amy ' Winehouse and Ray LaMontagne have dipped into the classic R&B catalog to create their unique Motwon and Stax sounds Motown Berry Gordy founded the Motown record label during 1959 in Detroit; Michigan. Motown was named after Detroit's most successful product: motor cars. (i.e: the moniker Mo-Town/Motor Town). Employing R&B and pop qualities, Motown saw its greatest success from 1959-1972,and they used a small group of studio musicians to back each of their featured artists. This small group of musicians eventually became known as The Funk Brothers and helped define an ageless sound. The drummers of Motown were Benny Benjamin, Richard "Pistol" Allen and Uriel Jones: Stax Jim Stewart and Estelle Axton founded the Stax record label in 1957. Based in Memphis, Tennessee; Stax was pop-influenced R&B label that also (like Motown) used one group of musicians to back their entire artist roster This backing band was well-known under the name Booker T and the MG's Their drummer was the influential Al Jackson, Jr: The drummers of Motown and Stax understood that they needed to compose drum parts that fit the song and made people dance: Ex 5.1 Benny Benjamin Al Jackson; Jr; Ietoxl "Pistol" Allen MOTOWN Uriel Jones 43 Ooq

Unit SeveN reading | techNIQUe perfOrmance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts Ensemble figures based on 16th-note subdivisions are used in many styles of music; namely funk; R&B and rock When a tune is based on a template of 16th-note subdivisions, syncopated and complex rhythms are usually prevalent (namely on the "e" and the "ah" of all beats) When reading 16th note-based charts, you must be comfortable with beat placement on all partials of 16th notes: Beat Placement: The"e" and"ah" Before you begin to develop a 16th note-based ensemble figure set-up fill repertoire, you must first solidify your ability to properly place every partial within 16th-note subdivision: Once you subdivide (and internalize) the 16th-note subdivision, you should practice 16th-note beat placement within a groove: In Unit 1, downbeats and upbeats were addressed, thus, the focus in this unit will be the "es" and "ah's" of every beat: To create a realistic setting, you will play a four-bar phrase: three bars of time with a single 6th-note ensemble figure in bar 4. Each figure should be played in three different manners: 1) with one-handed 16th notes on the hi-hat; 2) with two-handed 16th notes on the hi-hat and 3) with eighths notes on the hi-hat One-Handed 16ths: The"e" of Beat Ex 7.1 With one-handed sixteenth notes on the hi-hat at 75 bpm Template Ensemble Figure Drum Set

Chapter 3: The 4/4 to Cut Time Modulation 4/4 to Cut Time: Overview In this example; the chart will go from I6th-note funk feel in 4/4 to a samba in cut time: The funk section is written in 4/4 in order to display 16th-note patterns, which are idiomatic to the style The samba section is written in cut time to notate the ensemble figures as simply as possible; using larger note values. Also, the samba is known as a two-bar pattern; writing it in 4/4 would mistakenly notate the samba as a one- bar pattern: The Chart The chart has two sections: letter A, which is twelve bars long (in 4/4) featuring a one-bar funk groove; and letter B, which is 16 bars long (in cut time), featuring a two-bar samba pattern: Both sections have ensemble figures that need to be set up and orchestrated: Ex 8.10 =84 Funk feel continue: .. 2. 7ZA2 fine To samba Fill ~ 723 HZZ2 B Samba 7 continue__. 7 ) / To funk feel Fill Ctttttttttttt 7/ D. Unit fOUC reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Swung 16th-Note Grooves wIGhost Notes Soul-funk's primary focus is on memorable melodies alongside funk instrumentation, swinging grooves and strong; definitive backbeat: In addition, soul-funk places an equal emphasis on the professional craft of songwriting, jazz improvisation and funky grooves With its commercial accessibility, soul-funk produced some of the biggest stars of the 1960s, 70sand 2000s such as Stevie Wonder; Sly and the Family Stone, Amy Winehouse; The Roots, and Sharon Jones and The Dap Kings This week's chart is in the tradition of Stevie Wonder's classic recording period of the albums Innervisions and Songs in the Key € of Life. Structure: The AB 32-Bar Form This 32-bar form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter and B). Just as in previous weeks' units, each letter (section) represents three key musical attributes They - are (1.) particular rhythm; (2) a particular chord structure (and melody), and (3.) a specific phrase length: In this unit each section has the same phrase length: The A section is 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/first and second endings) , and the B section is also 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/ first and second endings) All together; these two sections make up the 32-bar form: For example: Ex 4.1 2 2 2_ Fill Switch to Ride cymbal B on repeat for Guitar solo 2 2 2_ D. UnIt ONE reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Soul-Jazz Soul jazz shuns the complex chord changes; walking bass lines and "swing" of the bebop era Instead, this new genre focuses entirely on syncopated grooves, repetitive bass lines and minimal chord changes: Musicians then craft their accompaniment; melodies and solos around these recurring elements: With the emergence of organist Jimmy Smith in 1956, soul jazz organ combos including a tenor; guitarist; drummer (and an occasional bassist) caught on and soulful players became stars, including Brother Jack McDuff; Jimmy McGriff; Charles Earland, and Richard "Groove" Holmes. Today's chart is in the style of John Scofield's organ based band and the funky soul swamp drumming of Idris Muhammad and Bill Stewart: Background: Lead Sheet A lead sheet is a sketch of a composition and it is usually provided to a small ensemble: Unlike a full blown chart; which contains every piece of musical information, a lead sheet is a modest chart that only specifies songs melody; chord structure; form and some basic information about the style (i.e: "medium swing") Each player within the group is expected to be proficient on his or her instrument; and know how to read a lead sheet. Together; these attributes help the player to create the appropriate musical part on their instrument: Ex 1.1 Straight I6th Funk _ 102 Intro Bb7(9) Drums Only Drums Onlv IS Es79) 2 Drumset Interpretation Lead sheets do not contain "beats" The drummer must create the appropriate feel (and corresponding sound) based on the style indicated (ballad, 2-feel; Latin, shuffle; etc:) Since a lead sheet is not a drum chart; it is neither necessary nor desirable for the drummer to play every figure (or rhythm) that is written within the music You must be much more selective when choosing which figures to play: Plaving time _through the majority of the figures and marking _the form should be thenorm: Fills and fill markers are not written in either: As a result; it is also up to the drummer to decide when (and if) a transition fill is necessary: Therefore, your own personal interpretation and creativity are vital in shaping the music Lastly, it is important to note that you must still observe the principles that we have discussed in the last 3 Drum Performance books: time, feel, note durations, and marking the form: Drum Performance 4 Bb7(9)

Unit Chapter 2 Soul-Jazz Sound As stated earlier; this chart (although a funk tune) is coming from the jazz tradition and perspective Therefore, your drumset approach and sound should come from this angle. Please use the following articulations: 1 Open "Non-Buried" Bass Drum: This will mimic the small legato bass drums used in jazz If you bury the beater; you will sound more like contemporary fusion than soul (or jazz) . 2. Snare Drum "Light Rim Shot": You can use rim shots within this tune; but they should not be "rock" rim shots They should be at a lower dynamic, used to get the drum to "speak"_~rather than just for volume: In addition, feel free to experiment with playing the center of the head or off- center (no rim shot) This will help you to sound more authentic like funk-jazz greats Idris Muhammad; Bill Stewart; Jabo Starks and Clyde Stubblefield: 3. Loose Hi-Hat: The hi-hat should not be too clean and tight in this style Try to make it a tad loose and dirty like older New Orleans jazz, soul and funk recordings If it is too clean you will sound more pop-fusion than jazz

Unit wo Unit Chapter 3 reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: New Orleans Second Line; 7/4 and 5/4 Funk This lead sheet is in the style of John Scofield's jazz group and the drumming of Bill Stewart This lesson will focus on contemporary interpretations of New Orleans second-line rhythms and jazz-funk grooves We will also explore phrasing these items in 7/4 and 5/4. Background: Lead Sheet Interpretation 2 As we learned in Unit 1, a lead sheet is a modest; sketch chart that only specifies a song's melody; chord structure; form and some basic information about the style (for example; "medium swing"). However; in this unit's lesson we will combine this new information with more traditional concepts from AABA and ABAB forms (Drum Performance books 1 and 2). Thus, each letter will now receive a different rhythm, a specific phrase-length and a particular melody and chord structure There will also be two structure additions: an open vamp at the beginning of the chart and a coda: Each of these items is now present in the lead sheet: For example: Ex 2.1 165 Swung Sths Second Line Feel Vamp Till Cue Open vamp 8 AZ E7 A7 Letter A: On cue, four bars repeated: B Bb7 Eb Letter B: three bars in 5/4,one bar in 7/4. UNIt five reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: R&B 16th-Note Funk A sub-style of R&B, funk drumming came in to prominence in the mid-60s to mid-7Os via the popularity of artists/bands such as James Brown, Tower of Power; Sly and the Family Stone and Cold Blood The funk style of drumming incorporates syncopated bass drum patterns which are placed under layered hi-hat and snare patterns: Many ' contemporary artists including Justin Timberlake and Christina Aguilera use elements ofthis classic style Thus, it is important for young drummers to develop this style This chart utilizes a basic AB form with an intro and coda, alongside specific grooves that are based in the funk genre: Let's take a look in detail: Intro The intro is a short; two-bar section that features a guitar setup. The drums enter on beat 4 of the second measure with a common; consecutive I6th-note figure: Ex 5.1 ReB Funk 107 Note: As this tune begins with the guitar player performing alone, you must make sure that he/she starts at the correct tempo of 107 BPM With this in mind, there are two methods to achieve this goal: (1.) Count them in with a traditional count-in ("1, 2, 1, 2, 3,4"), or (2) provide the tempo by clicking your sticks (or tapping the hi-hat) This secondary option allows the guitar player to begin at the exact moment that - they are ready to perform: Either method works, but you should discuss this with your bandmates before preforming the tune to settle on an approach: Also, once the tune begins,you need to make sure you are listening to the guitar player and feeling the time before you enter during bar 4. Letter A Letter A is a 12-bar section (with the use of first; second and third endings) that employs a one-bar drum pattern: There is also an ensemble figure on the "ah"of beat 4 in the first and second endings Ex 5.2 1.2. 13. Unit 8 - Chapter 3 Unit Ne reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Funk-Rock; Extended Fills; Metric Modulation This unit's chart expands on many of this books lessons by incorporating additional ensemble figures, extended fills and metric modulations into familiar drum grooves. Upon completing this lesson, you will have acquired the needed vocabulary and experience to play nearly any straight-eighth or 16th song utilized within a modern pop; rock or funk modulated context: Letter A Letter A features a unique two-bar phrase alongside a very syncopated ghost-note groove: Many of the bass drum notes are in unison with the snare drum and phrased in groups of two. For example: Ex 9.1 A Bass Drum Doubles: Even Placement and Sound Ex 9.2 You must pay close attention to these groupings and perform them with an even time placement and articulation: All bass drums notes (whether single or double) must have the same articulation and sound: If you do not do this, the bass drum notes will sound "muddy" "sloppy" and lack conviction: **With this in mind, make sure that you practice this groove slowly, while subdividing 16th notes Letter A: Bar 8 Ensemble Figure In measure 8,there is a short fill into two ensemble figures This acts as a pickup fill into the new drumset groove at letter B. For example: Ex 9.3 Fill ) Here is an idea for orchestrating this bar: Ex 9.4 Fill 25 82 Drum Performance 4

About the Authors Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues. At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel: In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom 96 Drum Performance 4

Chapter 3: Basic Independence The study of coordinated independence is essential in order to gain command and control of the drumset: The method presented in this (and upcoming units) introduces you to a "tried and true" method to begin developing the control needed to perform at a high level on your instrument To acquire these skills, you must develop disciplined and meticulous practice method There is no substitute for practicing slowly and in a controlled manner In these exercises, the ride cymbal and hi-hat will play a continuous pattern while the snare drum and bass drum are placed in various downbeat and upbeat groupings The cymbal pattern is a common pattern used in all genres of music which is a quarter note on beats 1 and 3 and two eighth notes on beats 2 and 4. In addition, the hi-hat is played with the foot on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 1.9 Ride cymbal Hi-hat with foot When played as straight eighth notes this pattern can be used in Latin, funk; rock, R&B, metal, etc: This common pattern can also be interpreted as swung eighth notes for swung styles such as jazz and hip-hop: As presented in Drum Performance 3, swung eighth notes may be indicated at the top of a chart with a symbol indicating all written eighth notes are swung unless otherwise indicated. For example: Ex 1.10 J-r;_ S Swung eighth notes indicator Equals General Rules There are a few general rules to keep in mind when beginning independence exercises 1 . When the snare drum (or bass drum) lands on a downbeat; it will always be paired with the ride cymbal. Ride cymbal and snare drum together on every downbeat Ex 1.11 Drum Technique 3

Unit 1 Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Over-the-Bar Groove Technique As you have learned from the Drum Performance series, drummer must be able to interact and communicate (musically and visually) with the rest of an ensemble. While technique does not necessarily tellyou what to play (how to interact appropriately with developed musical ideas), technique does provide you with the facility to be creative and play what comes to your mind in an instant: Common genres that allow for expansive interaction and creativity are jazz, funk, gospel, Latin and jam bands (Phish, John Scofield, String Cheese Incident and Widespread Panic) While a drummer must maintain steady groove, they must also be able to interact without harming the integrity of the groove Over-the-Bar Patterns To play"over the bar" means to play a repeated musical phrase (usually grouped in an odd amount of beats) against the grain of a groove template. Playing small groupings of ideas that do not land on beat 1 of a bar is a great technique to help you develop creative freedom: The dotted eighth note is a very useful rhythm to play in 4/4 that will help you begin to feel over-the-bar concepts. Play quarter notes with both feet while your hands play unison dotted eighth notes (or tied eighths and 16ths) between the floor tom and snare drum: The dotted eighth note is worth one and a half beats (or three I6th notes) Therefore; when repeated this pattern creates a three beat phrase which lands on downbeat every three beats. To start; we will use a two bar phrase as follows: Ex 1.9 Three beats Three beats Hands Feet In the above example; the three-beat phrase repeats on beat 4 of the first measure, creating an over-the- bar phrase To take this concept a step further; lets apply the same three-beat phrase to a four-bar phrase; as follows: Ex 1.10 Hands Feet

I6th Note Conversion The sticking from the six variations from above (Ex. 5.1 and 5.2) can also be played over 16th notes This application with open up your ability to play creative fills in rock and funk feels. This also creates an asymmetrical grouping of three I6th notes Therefore; this pattern will go over the bar We'll start with variations stopping before the pattern goes over the bar as follows: Ex 5.3 Right handed 3,, L LR   R L LR 3  LR R L UR   R [ UR   R L UR  R E  ,) L LR R L LR R L UR R [ &R Ex 5.4 Left handed 7= F8F3S R   R ~R @ L  R   r L = 1 R   Rl L R   RL L R   RL R   R L R   RL R) L R  RL 48 Drum Technique 4

Kevin is graduate of the acclaimed University of Miami jazz department with a BA in Studio Music and Jazz Performance. Since arriving at Musicians Institute in 2009, Kevin has been known as one of the instructors to go to in order to get your groove together: Kevin teaches Private Lessons, Drumset Performance, Music Minus One,   along with New Orleans Drumming a class which he created for Ml which covers the history ofthe drum set and the role of the instrument in jazz, R&B, rock & roll, funk and beyondl Kevin is proud to endorse these fine companies: Sabian Cymbals, Vic Firth Drumsticks, Remo Drumheads and Protection Racket Cases: 94 Drum Technique 4

CD Track 12  Saturday Night in Memphis This song features some nice horn playing: The bass guitar lays down some slick, melodic grooves. There are some nice trumpet figures being played during the sax solo. Support them in your groove: The song builds dynam- ically until a nice ensemble horn run at the end of section C2. There is plenty happening in this tune, so listen closely and funk it up. TiPS: 1. Lock in really tight with the bass guitar: Notice the staccato and legato figures: 2. Pay attention to the crescendos in the song: 3. Catch the ensemble figures on the last bar of section C2. 4. Keep the horn shots tight-no room for ringing cymbals. 5_ Notice the unpredictable ending, and catch those shots_ Gzooves: @aSy A1 Try the following grooves to comp the horn figures in section A1: Ai 19 A1 19 A2 1

CD Track Burn the Bridge The opening track on the CD starts off with a great funky syncopated bass line_ The A and B section beats are two-bar patterns. This song doesn't require many fills. It'$ important to lock in with the bass player to make this song groove. Have fun with this one. TIPS: 1. Play the tune first without trying to play any fills; get a feel for the form (don't get lost). 2. The bass drum is anticipated at the end of the Znd bar of letter A 3 . Notice the 7-bar pattern in section B; its not 8_ 4. Watch your dynamics and work towards the build-up to section C 5_ Catch the ensemble figures in the last bar of the tune_ 6 Catch some of the horns figures, especially at the end of section € The grooves are lettered to match the section of the song in which they belong: For example, groove A (in all sections from easy to advanced) belongs in section A of the song: Grooves: Easy A Jx ONLY B Jx ONLY C 19

jazz.text: Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Slow 12-Bar Blues The blues is one of America's greatest musical treasures. A roots music form that evolved out of African- American work songs, field hollers, spirituals,and country string ballads more than a century ago, the blues is the foundation of virtually every major American music form born in the ZOth century, including jazz, rhythm and blues, rock 'n'roll, and hip-hop. This unit's chart will focus on modern master of blues: Stevie Ray Vaughan: Structure: 12-Bar Blues (I-IV-V) The term "12-bar blues" refers to the number of measures, or musical bars, used to express the theme of a typical blues song: A 12-bar blues is divided into three four-bar segments A standard blues progression typically features three chords based on the first written as first (I), fourth (IV), and fifth notes ofan eight- note scale: In this case; the (B7) chord dominates the first four bars, the IV (E7) chord typically appears in the second four bars and the V (F#7) chord is played in the third four bars Ex 6.1 All B7 24 37 7 7 IV 3 3 E7 2044/ B7 V F#7 E7 B7 F#7 04 Although the 12-bar blues is broken up into four four-bar phrases that outline the |-IV-V chord structure; it is important to realize that you are ultimately dealing with a 12-bar phrase: Rehearsal Letters Just like the 32-bar AABA and ABAB are performed in 32-bar increments; this new structure will also get repeated in its phrase length of 12 bars: In this chart each set of 12-bars is labeled asan A section with each new 12-bars having a numeric suffix For example: Ex 6.2 Al A2 A3 A4 51

Unit Seven reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Shuffle 12-Bar Blues Todays chart will focus on a standard blues rhythm: the shuffle: Structure Il: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) Open Form: The 12-bar blues is considered an open form. This means that the song (or chart) is played (and subsequently repeated) in 12-bar increments without a specifically stated ending: Therefore; the 12-bar form (chart) will be performed in 12-bar increments (12-24-36-48-60-72 bars): With this fact in mind; there are two additional terms that modify, add to and accompany this form: They are: (1) the head and (2) a chorus Lets take a look at each in greater detail: The Head The term 'head" is most often used in jazz or blues-related contexts, and it refers to the thematic melody within a song's form. This melody is usually played at the beginning and at the end of a song's performance: Thus, if a bandleader requests one head (in a 12-bar form), that would mean that the band would play through 12 bars ofthe melody. For example: Ex.7.1 Melody Line Swing J = 120 F7 3 Bb7 F7 Bb7 F7 C7 € 3 Take Coda on cue Jazz and blues musicians also often give a signal (or cue) for the "head" or "top" by patting their hand on top of their head, which is meant to make sure everybody "goes back to the melody" the next time the "top of the form" comes around: 61

A Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus which refers to "the hook" of a song ~a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form" Thus,if a bandleader says they: want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12-bar blues form), that would be one 12-bar blues form of guitar solo. For example: Ex 7.2 Guitar Solo through the form IV IV V_ 10 Head and Chorus Terms Used Together Oftentimes a bandleader will state the form at the beginning ofa song He or she might say something like this: Were going to play one head, one chorus of guitar solo and one head out" Thus (in a 12 bar form), they want the band to play 12 bars ofthe melody; 12 bars (one time through the 12-bar form) of guitar solo and one 12-bar melody to the end. For example: Ex 7.3 Head (12 bars) One Chorus of Guitar Solo (12 bars) Head Out (12 bars) 62 Drum Performance

Unit 7 Chapter 1 Cue: As stated within the open form terminology, there is no specific ending within this form. Thus, a cue is employed. A cue is a notice made from one band member to the rest of the band in order to signal a break; rest or ending: Thus, in the chart you will see the text "on cue" This will tell you to get the band's attention by playing a short fill or counting them into the coda ending: Coda As definied in Unit 4,a coda is a specific ending; which is represented by this symbol (left). Therefore; after your cue; the band will take the coda sign after bar 8, and follow it down to the actual coda (marked by the same symbol) This is where the chart will end. For example: Ex 7.4 Bb7 A7 Ab7 3 G7 CzsuS4) C7 C#7 C7 7 Take Coda on cue F? Fi7   F7 5 Shuffle Feel: A shuffle feel is a triplet-based groove written in 4/4 time It is very common in blues songs, medium tempo jazz pieces and in rock music The shuffle usually refers to a "broken triplet feel;" where there is no middle triplet present: For example: Ex 7.5 3 It is also usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 7.6 3 Played as eighth note triplets This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as a "broken" eighth-note triplet: 63

About the Authors Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues. At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel: In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom

Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician: lyouug At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree, Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings; tours and shows After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles; maintaining a steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists'lists Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students. Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves, Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards. 88 Drum Performance

About the Authors A native of Denver; Colorado, Ryan Brown has   played drums and percussion since he was 11 years old, when he made the fateful switch from trumpet in elementary school band. He spent countless hours in his parents'basement over the next seven years honing his craft: He then headed to Indiana University; where he studied with rock legend Kenny Aronoff and graduated with Bachelor of Music degree in jazz studies and percussion, earning the university's prestigious Performer's Certificate as well; During and after college; Ryan did tours playing on the S/S Norway for Norwegian Cruise Line; sharpening his jazz performance and reading skills and seeing the world He moved to Los Angeles in 1999,and worked his way up in the music scene; becoming one of the busiest live and session drummers in town: In addition to his gig as the drummer for Zappa Plays Zappa, Ryan plays with Los Angeles-based bands Black Belt Karate and Madras, and in the bands of artists Arrica Rose, Keram Malicki-Sanchez and Space Oddity, among others. He has performed with many prominent artists, including Taiwanese pop star Chyi- Chin; Ingrid Michaelson; Niki Haris, Frank Stallone and the Norwegian Wind Ensemble: Ryan played drums on thehit single "The Real Thing"from Bo Bice, and can be heard on releases by Foreigner Circus Diablo, Hannah Montana, Clay Aiken, Cassie Davis, Madame Mayhem, Nick Lachey, Jesse McCartney, Heather Graham, Suzie McNeil (Rock Star INXS) and Stephanie McIntosh; as well as on the movie soundtracks for Kung Fu Panda (Hans Zimmer and John Powell), The Dark Knight (Hans Zimmer) and Ice Age: Continental Drift (Adam Schlesinger) . He's performed on recordings with Alex Lifeson, Earl Slick; Elliot Easton and Rudy Sarzo: He is member of the drum faculty at Musicians Institute, and helped write the school's new drum curriculum as well: 123
