Unt twO reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Punk-Pop Punk-pop is a post-grunge sub style of alternative rock that combines popular music melodies, chord changes and grooves with speedy punk tempos and loud, driving guitars Green Day and Weezer were the first bands to popularize this variation of rock in the 1990s, and there are several groups that play this style today: These include Fall Out Boy; Franz Ferdinand and Panic at the Disco. Structure: The ABAB Form This chart employs a musical structure common to rock; pop and jazz music: the ABAB form: It is comprised of two different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B) Both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes: a different rhythm (or groove), an eight-bar phrase and a particular riff and chord structure Let's examine each key element individually: 1.A different rhythm (or groove). As stated earlier; there are two main sections within this form and B) As a result; there are two grooves present within this chart: For example: The A section features a two-bar; straight-eighth hi-hat groove alongside a quarter-note (four-on-the-floor) bass drum pattern. There is also an open hi-hat accent on the "&"of each beat Ex 2.1 Medium Hi-hat The B section features a two bar; double-time feel, which placesthesnare drum on the "&"of each beat (rather_thanbeats Zand 4) The hi-hat is comprised of eighth notes and the bass drum employs 6th notes; which do not always line up with the hi-hat: Ex 2.2 Double Time Feel B Sloshy- 1/2 Open Hi-hat 15

Chapter 3: Performance Notes and Sound This classic R&B sound utilizes the same balance of sound used in rock and pop, but none of the kick; snare and hi-hat voices should ever overpower the groove. In general, the sounds should be warm and blend together evenly Thus, the optimum sounds are as follows: Snare sound: center snare Bass drum: off the head and open: Hi-hat: light and even Letter A In letter A the hi-hat should be very tight and quiet: You can use the tip of the stick or the upper part of the shaft just under the tip to obtain this sound: For example: Ex 5.6a Upper shaft (just below the tip) Tip supca JAZZ The cross-stick sound (on the snare drum) at letter A should be very clear and bright; which is accomplished by striking the rim between two lugs with the butt end of the stick As stated in Unit 3,this will get you in the general area and enable you to get a solid cross-stick sound: Remember; each snare drum is unique and has its own "sweet spot"Therefore; it is extremely important to experiment and find the correct ratio of "on-"and "off-the-drum" positions Ex 5.6b Letter B In letter B, the "all downbeats" snare drum part should be a center-snare sound with all notes played with the same velocity (do not accent 2 & 4).The bass drum should be played off the head and should blend with the bass player The hi-hat should be played slightly stronger than the A section: This can be accomplished by taking a tiny bit of pressure off of the hi-hat pedal with your foot: 46 Drum Performance 2

Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Jazz Jazz was created at the beginning ofthe ZOth century by blending rich African rhythms with sophisticated European harmony (chord structure): Improvisation and group interaction are two of the key elements that make jazz a great vehicle for creative drumming: This unit's chart will focus on a jazz interpretation of the blues, which allows us to focus on the jazz ride cymbal pattern, kick and snare comping, and a 12-bar drum solo Structure: 12 Bar Blues (I-IV-V) As mentioned in Performance 1, Unit 6,the most common musical form of blues is the 12-bar blues. In this case (because we are in the key of G), the (G7) chord spans the first four bars; the IV (C7) chord typically appears in the second set of four bars and the V (D7) chord is played in the third set of four bars. For example: Ex 6.1 G 1 D GI D. C al Coda Jazz Terminology Review As mentioned in Performance 1,_ Unit 7,there are a few key - terms and concepts related to jazz and blues music that are essential to understand. Open Form The term open form means that the song is played (and repeated) in 12-bar increments, without a specifically stated ending: Chorus Unlike a pop music chorus, which refers to "the hook" of a song,a jazz or blues chorus is a term that is slang for "one-time through the form:" If a bandleader says they want to play one chorus of guitar solo (in a 12- bar blues form) , that would be mean one pass through the 12-bar blues form; but with a guitar solo (in place of the melody). Cue Jazz and blues musicians often give each other the "head" or"top" cue by patting their hand on top of their head,which is usually meant to make sure everybody goes back to the melody the next time the top e ofthe form comes around; 52 Drum Performance 2

Unit 6 - Chapter 1 The Head The term head refers to the thematic melody within a song's form. The head for this chart contains many syncopated and recurring swung-eighth-note rhythms For example: Ex 6.2 G? 24 4EEEN G7 "E8 34} D7 G7 D. C al Coda Swing Eighth Notes As mentioned in Performance 1, Units 6 and 7, jazz and blues are usually written (in chart form) as eighth notes, although triplets are implied. This is recognized at the beginning of the chart with the following symbol: Ex 6.3 =JeJ This lets the reader of the chart know that every time they read an eighth note, they are to perform it as & "broken" eighth-note triplet: This. applies to the notated drum solo as well: 53

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The Jazz Balance of Sound In previous lessons, we have focused on a pop-rock balance of sound This places the main emphasis (and strong dynamic) on the kick and snare; while having the hi-hat placed quite below these within our "players mix" However; in jazz this balance is reversed. It features the ride cymbal as the main (loudest) voice; thehi- hat a close second, with the snare just below the hi-hat and the bass drum as the softest: For example: Ex 6.4 Loudest Pop-Rock Jazz Balance of Sound Balance of Sound Bass Drum* Ride Cymbal Hi-Hat Snare Drum* 70 30 40 Snare Drum Ju Hi-Hat E0 70 Bass Drum Softest *Note: bass drum and snare drum are equal in the pop-rock setting: The Ride Cymbal: The Foundation of a Good Jazz Time Feel The foundation of jazz drumming is the ride cymbal: The essence of a good jazz cymbal rhythm is the quarter-note pulse;as shown in example 6.5.As soon as you can make that feel good, move on and add the skip note to complete the full jazz ride pattern in example 6.6. It is important to maintain a strong quarter note pulse when adding the skip note: Ex 6.5 Ex. 6.6 -J trip let 2   trip let trip let trip let 54 Drum Performance 2

Unit 6 - Chapter 2 The normal playing area will be approximately halfway between the edge of the cymbal and the edge of the bell This playing area will give a nice blend between definition and wash: Closer to the bell equals more definition. Closer to the edge equals more wash: It is also important to note that the cymbals typically used in jazz playing are thinner than those used in rock or pop music With these facts in mind, the ride cymbal pattern should create a large blanket of sound, which covers the entire band and connects them all in sound. This does not mean the cymbal should be loud but it must be strong: Ex 6.7 Crash Accents: "Shouldering" the Ride In rock music; accents are "crashed" on a dedicated crash cymbal In jazz accents are played on the ride cymbal; but they are "shouldered" with the stick This is accomplished by using the shoulder ofthe stick to strike the top surface ofthe cymbal. Not only does this create a full washy sound; but it also helps to control the overtones of the cymbal, as well as making your playing sound more authentic Ex 6.8 The Hi-Hat: The Jazz Backbeat In straight-ahead jazz drumming; the hi-hat is typically played on counts 2 and 4 of the bar This is very similar to pop music's backbeat function There are at least three techniques that may be employed to play the hi-hat with your foot: 1) the rocking motion 2) bouncing the whole leg 3) flat-footed. Use whichever is most comfortable to you: Ex 6.9 J 55

Bass Drum: Feathering for the Choruses of Guitar Solo Feathering is playing the bass drum on straight quarter notes atan extremely quiet dynamic This articulation should fall underneath each of the other drumset voices. This technique was developed to add an attack and punch to the jazz upright-bass walking feel. The feathered bass drum should be "felt but not heard" If you can hear the bass drum, it is too loud. In order to achieve this sound; use the felt side of the beater and choke up on the pedal by placing your toes just behind the chain (or strap) of the footboard: Do not bring the beater back too far as it will make the stroke too loud. The beater should only come back about to 1-1/2 inches from the head. Ex. 6.10 (right) Important: You will e only feather the bass drum during the choruses of guitar solo. The bass drum strikes exactly with the ride cymbal on counts 1,2,3 and 4 The bass drum, hi-hat; and ride cymbal strike together on counts 2 and 4. You may want to record yourself playing this pattern to make sure that none of the voices are flamming: Ex 6.11 Comping Comping is short for"accompanying" Comping is how the rhythm section instruments, such as piano, guitar; and drums, support both the head (melody) and the soloist (choruses of solo) In each section of the form, comping takes on a different role. Let's take a look at both variations: Comping the Head During the head,the drums support the melody line by accompanying and accentuating notes within each phrase. These can be accented by either the snare drum or bass drum. However; the ride cymbal pattern and foot hi-hat must keep their patterns unchanged For this reason, it is very important to develop the proper independence to keep the ride and hi-hat going while playing various accents with the snare and/or bass drum: Let's take a look at bars 1-4 from both the head and drumset chart You will notice that the bass drum and snare drum comp the written guitar melody, with the ride and hi-hat unchanged: For example: Ex 6.12 GI # Important: For this unit; the bass drum does not feather during the head (melody) 56 Drum Performance 2

Unit 6 - Chapter 2 Comping the Choruses of Guitar Solo During the choruses of solo, the snare drum and bass drum accompany and react to the soloist This is not the time to display complicated jazz independence exercises underneath your ride cymbal and hi-hat patterns: However; independence is needed to properly execute many of the most tasteful and musical phrases. Therefore; listed below are 12 examples of basic comping patterns for your review: Play the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4.These patterns can be played on both the snare drum and bass drum as well or you can mix them up between the bass drum and snare drum: Comping Examples Ex 6.13 Jazz Drumming: Marking a Song's Form A drummer's most important job is to play a great groove with steady time However; these attributes are not enough to make music. We have another very important duty to perform: marking a songs form. Marking form is outlining (and drawing attention to) each different section within a song: Here; these sections are the head and chorus ofthe 12-bar blues This is accomplished by employing sonic changes to the drumset by changing ride cymbals for each new section of the song: (In jazz, all cymbals are considered rides ) The head should have a particular cymbal/texture and the choruses of solo should have different cymbal/texture: In today's chart; the following form, performance instructions and cymbal will be used: Ex 6.14 1_ Head (ride cymbal 1) Comping the head (melody) Ride Cymbal #1 2 Chorus of guitar solo (ride cymbal 2) Ride Cymbal #2 3. Chorus 2 of guitar solo Comping with the soloist and feathering the bass drum 4 Chorus of drum solo (You will prepare this for the second class of the week:) One 12-bar drum solo with stick-shot accents in bars 5-8 5_ Head ~taking the coda (ride cymbal 1) Ride Cymbal #1 57

Chapter 3: The Drum Solo Jazz drumming has a strong tradition in improvisation. This unit's lesson also features a 12-bar drum solo that clearly demonstrates many of the concepts brought forth by Philly Joe Jones; Max Roach and Art Blakey: Sound Addition: Stick Shot In bars 5-8 and bar 12 ofthis solo, a stick shot is used. A stick shot is usually performed by placing the left stickata 45-degree angle (to the drumhead) Thereafter; you must immediately press the bead (of the stick) into the drumhead: You should apply enough pressure to avoid a buzz sound and slightly mute the head as well The right stick then strikes the left stick and a wood-tone accent is heard This articulation is very common in jazz solo phrases and transition fills (Both traditional and matched grip can be used for this technique) Ex 6.15 Jazz Drum Solo The hi-hat must play on 2 and 4 Ex 6.16 J-J 37> 7E3 R L R R L R R   R 3 37> 3 377 3 3 377 3 58 Drum Performance 2 3 7

Unit SevEN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Bossa Nova Bossa nova is a style of Brazilian music popularized by Antonio Carlos Jobim, Vinicius de Moraes and Joao Gilberto. The bossa nova movement (which is Portuguese for "new trend") lasted six years (1958-63),and it contributed a number of songs to the standard jazz repertoire Today's chart will focus on a classic bossa nova drumming method, which is characterized by continuous eighths on the hi-hat (or ride cymbal), a typical two-bar cross-stick pattern and the bass drum playing eighth notes on "1-83-&1" Ex 7.1 Bossa Nova Notice that this is a two-bar cross-stick phrase The first bar has the pattern being played on beat 1, the"&" of 2 and beat 4.The second bar has the cross-stick pattern on beats 2 and the"&"of 3.It is also important to keep the bass drum pattern consistent; without any rhythmic variations AAB Open Form The form of this chart is AAB with each section being eight bars in length: This form is open (similar to the open 12-bar blues form), which means the AAB (24-bar) form circulates after the head (melody over AAB the first time) for solo choruses. With these facts in mind; the normal usages of the head and chorus (from Unit 6) still apply: Head = melody Chorus one time through the form Form Addition: Four-Bar Intro Vamp Unit 7's chart has a four-bar introduction that augments the AAB form: During the introduction, the drums continue to play the bossa nova ostintato while opening the hi-hat on the "&"of beat 4 and on beat 1_ Ex 7.2 The phrase "vamp until cue"is used to instruct you to continue to play the four-bar intro until you receive a visual cue (usually a confirming head nod). Since the guitar plays the melody at A,the guitar player will be in charge of giving the cue You must make sure you are keeping an eye on the guitar player during the intro. The four-bar intro may be played for varying lengths of time the song is performed, but the duration will always be a multiple of four (8, 12, 16,20,etc ). Ex 7.3 Bossa _ 108 Vamp_ until cue After Cue 5 62 Drum Performance 2

Unit 7 Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound As you have seen in the last unit's jazz lesson, we featured the ride cymbal as the main (loudest) voice; the hi-hata close second,with the snare is just below the hi-hat and the bass drum as the softest: The bossa nova balance of sound is quite similar: In this new context; the overall dynamic is quite quiet; at a dynamic of mezzo piano (medium soft) The hi-hat and ride articulation will be the strongest voice; but they . should not be as strong as last week's jazz ride: In addition; the cross-stick sound will be equal to the snare drum volume position. For example: Ex 7.4 Loudest Bossa Nova Balance of Sound Ride Cymbal or Hi-Hat Snare Drum or Cross-Stick 70 J0 40 J0 60 Bass Drum 70 Softest Crash Accents:"Shouldering" Either the Ride or Crash Cymbal Just like in jazz, cymbal accents are "shouldered" However; in this new Brazilian context; these articulations can be played on either the ride or crash cymbals This is accomplished by using the shoulder of the stick to strike the top surface of the ride cymbal (Ex. 7.5) or the outermost edge' ofthe crash cymbal (Ex. 7.6). Not only does this create a full washy sound; but it also helps to control the overtones of the cymbal as well as making a more stylistically authentic sound. Ex 7.5 Ex. 7.6 63

The Hi-Hat: The Bossa Backbeat Just as in straight-ahead jazz drumming; the bossa nova hi-hat is also played on counts 2 and 4 of the bar: This is very similar to pop music's backbeat function. There are at least three techniques that may be employed: 1) heel up 2) heel down 3) bouncing the whole leg: Please use whichever is most comfortable for you: Ex 7.7 Important: You will play on the ride cymbal and close the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4 during the choruses of guitar solo Bass Drum: Proper Bossa Sound The bossa nova bass drum sound features the dotted-quarter/eighth rhythm at an extremely quiet dynamic (see example 7.4). This articulation should fall beneath all of the other drumset voices Please be careful to make both notes even in volume: Many drummers mistakenly accent the second note of each grouping; which is not authentic or appropriate. Ex 7.8 Correct Incorrect In order to achieve this quiet sound, use an open "unburied" stroke and the felt side of the bass drum beater Next; choke up on the pedal by placing your toes just behind the chain (or strap) of the footboard. Do not bring the beater back too far; this will make the stroke too loud The beater should only come back about 2 to 2-1/2 inches from the head. Ex. 7.9 The bass drum strikes exactly with the hi-hat (or ride cymbal) on counts "1-83-&1."You may want to record yourself playing this pattern to make sure that none of the voices are flamming: Also, the bass player plays this rhythm as well, so make sure to listen and lock in with the bass player: Ex 7.10 64 Drum Performance 2

Unit 7 Chapter 2 Comping Part 2: Duration Long Notes In the last unit's jazz example; the drums comped nearly all of the written melody: In this chart; the drums support the melody line by accompanying and accentuating the long notes within each phrase. In this context, the eighth notes and quarter notes would be considered short notes: Any half notes or tied notes (where the tie extends the duration of the note) would be considered a long note. For example: (S= short notes; L = long notes) Ex 7.11 Em7665 A7b9) Dmi A7*9) E S $ S L S L S L S S L Long Notes in Use: Now let's take a look at bars 1-4 from both the head and drumset chart: You will notice that the drums accentuate the long notes within the melody by playing "shouldered" crashes. These articulations mimic the long duration of the selected (tied) guitar notes Note: In this chart these long notes are highlighted with an accent purely to aid visually, and should not actually be hit harder The bossa pattern continues throughout: For example: Ex 7.12 A Dmi A7b9) Dmi A7*9) Comping the Choruses of Guitar Solo During the choruses of solo,you can play either the snare drum or cross-stick These voices can accompany and react to the soloist: This is not the time to display complicated Brazilian independence exercises underneath your bossa nova pattern: However; independence is needed to properly execute many of the most tasteful and musical phrases To start developing some basic independence in the left hand; below are a few examples of basic bossa nova comping patterns listed below: Please put - the hi-hat foot on beats 2 and 4. These patterns can be played with both snare drum and cross-stick Ex 7.13 65 Dmi Dmi Em7(b5) Dmi

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound As stated in previous lessons, many charts have focused on a pop-rock balance of sound This places the main emphasis (and strong dynamic) on the kick and snare while having the hi-hat placed quite below these within our "players" mix Reggae features this balance of sound, while adding a very strong cross-stick within the snare drum's dynamic range. For example: Ex 8.12 Pop-Rock Reggae Balance of Sound Balance of Sound Bass Drum Bass Drum Snare Drum 10 Snare Drum Cross-stick 30 40 : Hi-Hat Hi-Hat 60 770 Softest Crash Accents 1: Anticipated Just like jazz, reggae crash cymbal accents can be "anticipated" and played prior to the downbeat: This actually occurs on the last triplet of beat 4,and it is very common within a shuffled reggae style: This action occurs in three places in this chart: bar 12 leading into B, the fourth bar of B and the seventh bar of B (hi-hat this third time). For example: Ex 8.13 Bar 12 4th bar of B Zth bar of B 74 Drum Performance 2

About the Authors Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues. At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel: In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom 94 Drum Performance 2

Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician: leuug At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree; Stewart became a sought- after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows. After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles, maintaining a steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists'lists. Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years; Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves; Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards. 95

However; this does not mean you couldn't add a few fills to help shape this figure: For example: Ex 2.5 In the example above (Ex. 2,5), the drums are basically grooving through the first two eighth notes, using the snare drum backbeat to set up the"&"of beat 2 and then setting up the"&"of beat 3 with a double stop between the snare drum and floor tom. Sometimes, to create a dramatic effect; no set-up fill is warranted: A composer may want the band to kick in together with no hint of something coming: For example: Ex 2.6 In the example above (Ex 2.6) there are countless fill ideas that come to mind; but leaving the figure alone may be the best idea. Of course your decision may be made for you if there are fill indicators or directives written on the chart: For example: Ex 2.7 Fill CCCCC__- No filll Drum charts really do run the gamut; and in the end it is usually up to the drummer to decide what needs to be played: Here is a list of some possible types of charts you could be presented with in your career: Fully notated: Contains detailed drum notation with specific drum fills, orchestration performance directives and dynamics Skeletal: Provides the form and basic groove; possibly with some ensemble figures: Lead sheet: A chart that provides the form, chords, melody and basic feel (example: 3/4 jazz waltz) but no specific drum parts Piano, bass or trumpet chart: Many times the drum chart goes missing, and you'll be handed piano or bass chart (lead trumpet if in a big band situation) that at least provides the form and essential rhythms: Shorthand: Many times you may have to chart tune out yourself: This method has endless possibilities 20 Drum Reading 3

Unit {OUN reading | techNIQue performance Chapter 1: Basic Concepts In this unit; swung eighth-note ensemble figures (paired with larger subdivisions: quarter notes; half notes and whole notes) will be presented in a jazz (triplet-based) setting: Ifyou are doing a trio or quartet-style gig you will most likely be given a"lead sheet" chart which provides the basic form and feel but little more: If you are doing a big band gig then you are more likely to see a specifically notated drum chart with ensemble figures, articulations and notated fills. Ex 4.1 Eighth-Note Triplet Placement Because a triplet contains three eighth notes wedged into the space normally One occupied by two eighth notes, the distance between these notes changes: Straight eighth notes divide one beat symmetrically in half putting the Ex 4.2 upbeat equidistant from the downbeat: When there are eighth-note triplets, the third partial of the triplet is now your upbeat. This note will be further from the preceding downbeat but closer to the following downbeat: One Trip let Two Trip let In eighth-note triplet-based music; when seeing a freestanding eighth note on an upbeat; be sure to place the eighth note on the third partial= of the triplet: For example: Ex 4.3 S= 3 S= 3 One Two Three Four 43

The "Eighth-Note Rule" In jazz (and other eighth-note triplet ~based contemporary music), notation cannot always convey the actual phrasing or "feel" of a figure Therefore, the"eighth note rule"is a simple guideline that can assist you with ensemble figure interpretation: Basically,if you see a freestanding eighth note with nothing following it,then it will be accented. For example: Ex 4.4 When you see a string of eighth notes, the last one ofthe group is accented, with the others functioning a5 lead-in notes. For example: Ex 4.5 Lead in tones Accented last note of phrase If we take a guitar line and convert it into ensemble figures, the accents will naturally fall into place: For example: Ex 4.6 Swing 1 In the example above (Ex. 4.6), the end ofeach short phrase is accented along with the freestanding eighth notes You should catch the accents, but can avoid playing every written figure: Here is an example of how this would be voiced on the drum set within a jazz setting: Ex 4.7 44 Drum Reading 3 Swing

In addition, by using triplet fill-ins and snare drum rolls, we will also lookat other ways to orchestrate fills (and figures): Triplet Fill-Ins As you learned in Reading 2, it is essential to be able to read an eighth-note triplet-based figure, while also filling in consecutive triplets (i.e: either alternating or right-hand lead) . 3 Unit OUN reading | technIque | pertOrMance Chapter 1: Cut Time As you learned in Reading 1 , Unit 1,the time signature of 4/4 can also be represented by a large "C"in place ofthe 4/4 symbol,referring to "common time" This is simply due to the fact that a large percentage of music is in 4/4 time: Cut Time Cut time refers to the 4/4 time signature being "cut"in half, which is represented by a time signature of 2/2 or the symbol: € Cut time (or 2/2) would mean that there are two beats to a measure and the halfnote gets the beat: In the example below (Ex. 9.1) you can see the altered counting as follows: Ex 4.1 T Of course the above passage could be written in 4/4. It would look and sound the same but the counting would be different: For example: Ex 4.2 3 47 The need for cut time comes into play when the tempo is very fast (say, 175 bpm and higher) When needing to count"1-2-3-4"at fast tempos it is not uncommon for a musician to tense up and feel nervous. Cut time allows for counting to pass by at a slower rate (half) allowing the reader to relax: Instead of counting"1-2- 3-4"at 1 8Obpm,one can count"1-2"at 9Obpm There are many jazz and Latin tunes that are at ZOObpm and higher; which is why you will commonly see charts in this ilk written in cut time. Let's look at the examples from above (Ex. 9.1 and 9.2) with tempo markings: Ex 4.3 =100 Counting two beats per measure in cut time LdJdJEJ J] 4| &  2 a   2 200 Counting four beats per measure in 4/4 d S F3 1  & 2   &   3   & 4   & 1  & &   3 38 Drum Reading 4

S Unit Ne reading | techNIQUe pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Modulation Techniques As you learned in Unit 8, metric modulations can be used to facilitate smooth (and musical) tempo and feels changes within a chart: In this unit; two more common modulations are presented to help you familiarize yourself with the focal points and techniques used to professionally execute modulations: Modulations: Stylistically Correct Notation Modulations not only allow for tempo and feel changes, but they also allow the performer to be presented with the most common (and practical) notation possibilities available. For instance; there are certain feels and styles that are always written a certain way, i.e: jazz is written in 4/4 with the hi-hat on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 9.1 150 Tempo Swing feel Jazz ride cymbal pattern Hi-hat on beats 2 and 4 Although the examples below (Ex. 9.2) will sound correct to the listener (they would most likely be unaware of the count off; listed tempo and how the chart is notated) it would feel strange to count (and read) jazz in this manner: Ex 9.2 J = 75 Swing feel Tempo Hi-hat on every upbeat =75 Swing feel

As you can see, these examples (Ex. 9.2) place the hi-hat on every upbeat, which is not the correct way the hi-hat is felt in jazz Also, the notation for the jazz ride pattern is much more dense and complex than it needs to be: This stylistically incorrect notation will affect how The groove is interpreted, resulting in a groove that is stiff and flat Therefore; notation has a strong influence on how a groove is perceived by the performer: Another example of stylistically incorrect notation would be writing an up-tempo Latin chart in 4/4 as opposed to the preferred cut time Most Latin grooves are two-bar patterns and are felt in"2 "While it is not technically incorrect to notate an up-tempo Latin chart in 4/4,this is not the accepted standard, which can have an adverse effect on how the groove ultimately feels (and how ensemble figures are interpreted): The example below displays stylistically correct notation (or preferred notation) for a traditional mambo pattern with ensemble figures. The pattern spans two bars, the bass drum pattern lands on the "ah"of beat 1 and the "&" of beat 2 (remember that cut time is 2/2, two beats per measure) and the ride pattern and ensemble figures are simply written with larger valued notes (eighths; quarters and dotted quarters). This is correct notation for a mambo as follows: Ex 9.3 = 116 Mambo Two-bar pattern 2 Again;, although the next example below (Ex 9.4) is not "technically" (or mathematically) incorrect; it is not the preferred notation to use when writing up-tempo Latin charts For example: Ex 9.4 J-116 Mambo One-bar pattern Z73 90 Drum Reading 4

Unit fOUC reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Swung 16th-Note Grooves wIGhost Notes Soul-funk's primary focus is on memorable melodies alongside funk instrumentation, swinging grooves and strong; definitive backbeat: In addition, soul-funk places an equal emphasis on the professional craft of songwriting, jazz improvisation and funky grooves With its commercial accessibility, soul-funk produced some of the biggest stars of the 1960s, 70sand 2000s such as Stevie Wonder; Sly and the Family Stone, Amy Winehouse; The Roots, and Sharon Jones and The Dap Kings This week's chart is in the tradition of Stevie Wonder's classic recording period of the albums Innervisions and Songs in the Key € of Life. Structure: The AB 32-Bar Form This 32-bar form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter and B). Just as in previous weeks' units, each letter (section) represents three key musical attributes They - are (1.) particular rhythm; (2) a particular chord structure (and melody), and (3.) a specific phrase length: In this unit each section has the same phrase length: The A section is 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/first and second endings) , and the B section is also 16 bars long (with the aid of a repeat/ first and second endings) All together; these two sections make up the 32-bar form: For example: Ex 4.1 2 2 2_ Fill Switch to Ride cymbal B on repeat for Guitar solo 2 2 2_ D. Unit 4- Chapter 1 The Jazz Ride Pattern as Swung I6th Notes In this week's lesson, you will be applying the jazz ride pattern to the hi-hat voice as a swung I6th-note groove: In order to fully understand this concept; let's take a lookat the jazz cymbal pattern and how it can be applied and translated to a modern soul-funk groove: Here is the jazz ride pattern, traditionally notated as eighth-note triplets: Ex 4.2 Now; let's add snare comping ghost notes to the"&"of beat and beat 4,and a backbeat on beat 3: Ex 4.3 Next; lets phrase (and notate) this pattern as I6th-note triplets: Ex 4.4 Finally; let's"translate"this I6th-note triplet groove into swung I6th notes for this weeks new hi-hat and snare ghost note pattern: Ex 4.5 Now that you understand this concept, let's take a look at each section of the chart in detail: 35

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A Ex 4.6 Sound:"Bury" the bass drum Beater; snare rim shot and a light, even hi-hat: The intro and letter A feature a two-bar phrase alongside a very syncopated eighth and swung 16th-note based groove; which utilizes the snare drum, bass drum and closed hi-hat voices The hi-hat employs the jazz ride pattern as swung 16th notes, and the snare drum "swings"the same ghost-note pattern from Unit 2's B section: Again, these ghost notes are played extremely softly; which should be "felt rather than heard" Letter A Ghost Note Exercises: The Hands__Hi-Hat and Snare Drum Before attempting the groove at Letter A,it is extremely important that you understand how the snare drum ghost pattern fits within the swung I6th-note hi-hat pattern and pulse: Please make sure to practice this pattern slowly and evenly while subdividing both 16th-note triplets and swung I6th notes: Once it becomes comfortable, you should gradually work up to this weeks tempo of 91 BPM: Ex 4.7 Letter A Preliminary Hi-Hat and Ghost Note Exercises: Isolate Each Bass Drum At 91 BPM, it is very easy to accidentally rush these ghost notes; play them too loudly; flam them with the bass drum, and generally make the groove feel uneven: Therefore; we have compiled a set of preliminary exercises that will help you to isolate each bass drum note against the ghost note pattern: In order to play perfect unisons between the snare and bass drum; please make sure to subdivide these exercises within a 16th-note triplet click at a slow tempo before working up to 91 BPM: Ex 4.8 F 36 Drum Performance 3

Hand Position Many drummers play the same hi-hat and snare drum hand position for every groove. However; this cannot possibly work for every rhythmic pattern or sonic articulation. In order to effectively play a strong backbeat within this week's jazz ride hi-hat pattern, you may need to alter your normal "grooving" hand position: Rather than crossing your hands over each other in the normal position (Ex 4.13, Fig.3), move your hi-hat hand slightly off- center while keeping your elbows in. Next, strike the hi-hatat a slight angle (Fig.4). This will allow you to stay relaxed and still allow for your left hand to lift up and play a strong backbeat (Fig.5). Important: Do not move your right elbow outward away from your body. This will cause a great amount of tension and fatigue within your groove: (Fig.6) Ex 4.12 Fig: 3 Fig: " 4 Fig: 5 Fig: 6 38 Drum Performance 3

Therefore; we have compiled an additional set of preliminary exercises that will help you to isolate each bass drum note against the snare ghost note and jazz ride hi-hat pattern: Ex 4.14 Once you have these seven exercises at a comfortable tempo,you may practice two-bar phrases as follows: Ex 4.15 39

Unit 5 Chapter 3 Unit Six reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Slow Swing Grooves and Subdividing Swing blues is a jazz-tinged style of blues that came to prominence in the mid-to-late 1940s. Usually featuring a vocalist in front of a large; horn-driven orchestra, the style is earmarked by intensely shouted vocals,honking tenor saxophone solos and a driving rhythm section. The lyrics are celebratory in nature,and the music was the bridge between the older styles of small band blues and the big band jazz sound of the 1940s. Structure: 12-Bar Blues Form This 12-bar blues form is comprised of three different sections and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter: A, A2, A3. The head (and main riffs) appears within A and A2, and A3 is a 12-bar chorus of guitar solo. For example: Ex 6.1 60 Slow Swing ### Letter A: First chorus with breaks: SlA2| Letter A2: Second chorus with breaks: Also fourth chorus due to D.S. A3 Letter A3: Third chorus for guitar solo. D. S al Coda 50 Drum Performance 3

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound Letter A Ex 6.5 Sound: Open,"unburied" bass drum beater; snare cross-stick and a legato, open ride cymbal, Letter A features a one-bar phrase alongside an open; "unburied"four-on-the-floor bass drum groove, cross- stick backbeat and jazz ride cymbal pattern: The ride cymbal should propel the music forward; but because ofthe 60 BPM tempo it will be quite challenging to keep these notes from rushing; dragging or straightening out With this in mind, you must subdivide the groove in eighth-note triplets as follows: Ex 6.6 trip let 2 trip let 3 trip let 4 trip let Letter As 12-Bar Form Divided into Three Subsections Within letter As 12-bar head, there are three subsections: a stop-time introduction; four bars of groove, and ensemble figures Let's take a look at each in detail: Ex 6.7 1. Stop-Time Introduction: Bars 1-4 1 Bars 1-4 are considered the introduction of this 12-bar phrase Bars 1, 2 and 3 feature ensemble figures that should be played as written; without any setup fill or audible cue: Therefore; in order to play these in time, you must subdivide eighth-note triplets and count "1-trip-let; 2-trip-let; 3-trip-let; 4-trip-let" For example: Ex 6.8 trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let trip let Bar 4has an eighth-note drum build into a broken 16th-note fill This bass drum, snare and tom fill transitions perfectly into the groove portion (bars 5-8) of letter A Ex 6.9 52 Drum Performance 3

Unit 6 - Chapter 2 Letter A3 Ex 6.18 A3 Letter A3 continues the jazz ride groove; but now the snare drum features continual swung eighth notes In addition, the snare drum plays a slight rim shot on beats 2 and 4 Shuffle Note: Please make sure that the jazz ride and snare shuffle pattern is performed with accuracy, conviction and a strong backbeat: It is strongly suggested that you isolate this groove before running the chart If a proper shuffle is not achieved, the resulting guitar performance will suffer: The Guitar Solo: Letter A3, 12-Bar Form Divided into 2 Subsections Letter A3's 12-bar chorus of guitar solo has two subsections: eight bars of groove and four bars of ensemble figures within the groove The groove has been described in the previous paragraph, so let's take a look at the ensemble figures within bars 33-36. Ensemble Figures: Bars 33-36 Just as in the previous choruses, bars 33-36 have ensemble figures that are played within the groove: For Example: Ex 6.19 D. S al Coda Bar 24 has a 16th-note fill on beat which transitions perfectly into the D.S. and back to letter A2. When executing this fill, focus on the right hand which; when isolated, plays an eighth-note triplet: For example: Ex 6.20 55

Unit 6 Chapter 3 Unit SeveN reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Jazz (with Brushes) to Bossa Nova Latin-tinged jazz came to prominence in the early 1960s. Usually featuring a small, jazz-driven combo, this style is earmarked by uptempo, yet relaxed, winding melodies; sophisticated solos and a driving rhythm section This units chart will focus on combining (and tastefully transitioning between) a triplet-based jazz feel and a straight eighth-note bossa nova feel. Structure: AABA Form This 32-bar AABA form is comprised of three distinct choruses There are two choruses of the head, which feature a triplet jazz brush feel and a chorus of solo placed over a straight-eighth bossa nova feel The chorus of solo is also shared between the guitar and drums, with each alternating eight-bar solos Lets take a look at the entire form: Ex 7.a Swing Brushes First and second A sections: Swing/brushes 10 B section: Swing/brushes Head: Jazz (triplet) feel 18 Last A section: Swing/brushes Sct up Bossa fccl 22 59

Chapter 2: Performance Notes and Sound The Head As mentioned in earlier units, the "head" (in a jazz setting) refers to the melody of the tune The drums play standard jazz brush pattern* (Ex. 7.9, page XX) while comping the long/tied notes played by the guitar (which is playing the melody): Letter A Within the A sections (eight bars each), there are a few hits on the"&" of 4, plus a dotted quarter-note figure in bars 5 and 6. For example: Ex 7.3 Swing/Brushes 5 FF43F ZEE Recurring Dotted Quarter Figure The figure within bars 5-6are a very typical within jazz phrasing: This pattern is based on the dotted quarter notes (one and a half beats) and goes "over the bar line" superimposing a grouping of three over four: Try playing this figure as written, with the left hand staying on the snare and the right hand staying on the ride cymbal: For example: Ex 7.4 R R R Letter B During letter B, there are a few"pushes" on the"&"of 4.These should be played with a gentle; open bass drum sound under an accented/shouldered ride cymbal: Ex 7.5 B 4F 4 ' =F 62 Drum Performance 3

Unit 7 Chapter 2 Transition from Jazz to Bossa As we have previously studied, the jazz feel is based on the eighth-note triplet: In the last two bars of the head, there is a two-bar hold (notated with an extended whole note on bar 7) which gives you time to (1) switch from brushes to sticks and (2) set up the straight-eighth bossa feel for the solo sections: Ex 7.6 Set up Bossa feel Let's now examine how to manage all of this within two bars: Ex 7.7 Set up Bossa feel Set brushes down and pick up sticks. Play a very clear and precise straight eighth- Stay relaxed and do not rush this activity: note fill setting up the bossa. Musical Note: Setting up a New Feel and Subdivision Many (if not all) professional drummers properly set up the transition between swung and straight feels They accomplish this by playing a fill one bar before the new section, using the upcoming section's feel and subdivision This allows the drummer to properly"cue"the band that a new feel is about to occur: Ifyou fail to do this, the new section will appear"out of nowhere" and not sound musical: Here are a few setup ideas for the last bar of the head: Ex 7.8 Set up Bossa feel 63

Brushes: The Conventional Stroke The conventional stroke has the same rhythmic structure as the conventional jazz ride cymbal pattern, and the same timekeeping feel. It can be used for medium and uptempo jazz timekeeping: The Right Hand You should begin at the tail of the arrow, using the broken triplet as your pickup. Then move to the point (beat 1). Lift and return to the tail (beat 2). Repeat this pattern for beats 3 and 4. The Left Hand In a half-note rhythm and a clockwise direction; circle continuously by starting at the tail and moving towards the point (the first beat). Complete this circle at the tail by gently sweeping at the second beat: Ex 7.9 START StaAT Right Hand Slide Half-Circles on Snare Left Hand Note: As the left hand is executing the circular pattern, be careful not to "dig in" or press too hard on the drumhead: You can also give the brush a slight accent to help convey the time feel on beats 2 and 4. Also, the hi-hat closes on 2 and 4, matching the hands, which come together on the same counts The Solos The AABA chorus of solo is broken up into smaller increments as follows: The bossa nova solo section is one chorus long and the solos are subdivided as follows: First A section: Bass and drums play a bossa to accompany an eight-bar guitar solo. Second A section: Guitar and bass stop for eight-bar drum solo: B section: Bass and drums play a bossa to accompany an eight-bar guitar solo. Last A section: Guitar and bass stop for eight-bar drum solo. Drums set up jazz feel in final bars of solo: 64 Drum Performance 3

Setup 2: Last Two Bars of Bossa/Solo Section: Set Up and Establish the Triplet Jazz Feel Upon reaching the last two bars of the bossa/solo section, you must set up the upcoming D. S.al Coda return to the head. In order to successfully transition and set up the triplet jazz feel, you must play a triplet fill idea on the last bar of the solo. This commonplace within this style of chart; and is also explicitly stated in the chart as follows: Ex 7.12 Set up Swing feel D. C al Coda (repeats are good) Example: Setting Up a New Feel and Subdivision Ex 7.13 1 D. C al Coda (repeats are good) Bossa Solo Transition back to swing feel Play jazz time:. The Head (After the DC) Upon taking the D.C, you will return to A,staying on sticks,and now play the last head on the ride cymbal: Here is an example of a comping idea for the A sections during the D. $. Ex 7.14 Jazz/Bossa 158 Swing[Brushes (Sticks on D. S.) The Coda: Upon completing bar 30 ofthe head (after the DC), the chart concludes by taking the coda. The coda is comprised of bars 29 and 30 being repeated to serve as a turnaround into the final bar A turnaround is a short musical interlude repeated to signal the end of a tune- ~a common practice in jazz Ex 7.15 4 L   R L L  R L   R R  L L   R 66 Drum Performance 3

Unit eight reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Top 40 Pop with Samba Vamp "Top forty" is music industry shorthand for the most popular; best-selling and most frequently broadcast pop songs within a given week or month Over the years, it has also come to represent a popular song from particular era as well Thus, this play-along; called "Timber Grooves" is a dance-pop chart in the tradition Justin Timberlake and Michael Jackson. It features two popular grooves that you will definitely play on a contemporary"cover" gig: Structure: The AABA 32-Bar Form This study employs a classic musical structure common to rock; pop and jazz music: the AABA 32-bar form, which you have already seen in Drum Performance and 2. To review; this form is comprised of two different sections, and each one is assigned a rehearsal letter (A or B). In addition, both letters (sections) represent three key musical attributes: They are: a different rhythm (or groove) , an eight-bar phrase and a particular riff and chord structure So how does this information apply to this chart here in Drum Performance 32 Well; let's examine each key element individually: 1. A different rhythm (or groove). As stated earlier; there are two letters within this form, and each one now represents a particular rhythm (or groove). As a result; there are two grooves present within this chart: For example: The A section features a straight-eighth hi-hat groove alongside a syncopated 16th-note bass drum pattern: Not only is this groove a two-bar phrase; but it also features a tightly closed hi-hat (in both bars) , with an opening on the"&"of beat 3_ Ex 8.1 Light; Tight Closed Hi-Hat The B section groove is a one-bar phrase, which features a quarter-note hi-hat and bass drum pattern The hi-hat has an opening on the "&" of beat 4. Ex 8.2 B Louder Tight Closed Hi-Hat 70 Drum Performance 3

Unit 9 - Chapter 2 Letter C Ex 9.11 Swing 7 7 4X Sound: Open;"unburied" bass drum beater; snare drum rim shot and a legato, open jazz ride cymbal. Letter C is a 32-bar section; which features an uptempo jump swing shuffle within the jazz ride pattern: In addition; there is an ensemble figure on beat 4 in bar 8 (above): Please voice this accent within the groove as follows: Ex 9.12 Letter D Ex 9.13 D 3X Solo Solo: This seven-bar solo (within the eight-bar phrase of Letter D) will occur over the ensemble figures and the original swung-I6th New Orleans second line groove This is marked by notation that states that the quarter note now equals the eighth note (Ex. 9.13 above): Solo Break into the Fermata Cue: Upon reaching bar 8 of the drum solo, you will play an extended (open) solo marked by the text "solo break" Just after the "&" of 2, there are two slashes commonly known as "train tracks" This symbol tells you that there is a break/stop in the momentum of the normal time and tempo of the chart: Upon concluding your solo, you will cue the band into the final whole-note hit (and fermata) This is stated by the text cue band" Ex 9.14 Solo Break Cue Band: 83

About the Authors Donny Gruendler was born and raised in the diverse musical  surroundings of Detroit, Michigan: As result; Gruendler grew up alongside an unusually broad range of influences including soul, funk; pop, hip hop; traditional swing, hard bop; techno, house and blues. At age 20, Gruendler graduated from Berklee College of Music with Bachelor of Music degree At age 21, he earned his Master of Music degree from Wayne State University in Detroit: Now living in Los Angeles, Gruendler has performed, programmed; toured and recorded behind such artists as Kenny Burrell, John Medeski; DJ Logic the Funk Brothers, DJ A-Ski (Unique 74), Rick Holmstrom,and Kirk Fletcher: He has composed and/or played on jingles for Axe Body Spray; RE/MAX on the Boulevard, and NPR. His select film/TV credits include Last Holiday, Father of Invention, and the Showtime feature Chicago Overcoat: Donny is also a noted producer under the pseudonym Inc, and one half of the funky-soul-electro duo Rhett Frazier Inc: Okayplayer describes their productions as cosmic brilliance delivered via a well-stirred pot of soul, jazz, rock; funk and gospel: In the education realm, Gruendler is Vice President of Instruction and Curricular Development at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California, and he is also a member of the Modern Drummer education team and a frequent columnist for the magazine: Donny has also released several instructional books, DVDs and other online/digital content under his own name: For more information; please visit WWWdonnygruendlercom 94 Drum Performance 3

Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician: leuug At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree, Stewart became a sought- after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows. After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles, maintaining a steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the-point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists'lists. Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years; Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves; Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards. 95

UnIt ONE reading | techNIque pertOrMaNce Chapter 1: Intro to Soul-Jazz Soul jazz shuns the complex chord changes; walking bass lines and "swing" of the bebop era Instead, this new genre focuses entirely on syncopated grooves, repetitive bass lines and minimal chord changes: Musicians then craft their accompaniment; melodies and solos around these recurring elements: With the emergence of organist Jimmy Smith in 1956, soul jazz organ combos including a tenor; guitarist; drummer (and an occasional bassist) caught on and soulful players became stars, including Brother Jack McDuff; Jimmy McGriff; Charles Earland, and Richard "Groove" Holmes. Today's chart is in the style of John Scofield's organ based band and the funky soul swamp drumming of Idris Muhammad and Bill Stewart: Background: Lead Sheet A lead sheet is a sketch of a composition and it is usually provided to a small ensemble: Unlike a full blown chart; which contains every piece of musical information, a lead sheet is a modest chart that only specifies songs melody; chord structure; form and some basic information about the style (i.e: "medium swing") Each player within the group is expected to be proficient on his or her instrument; and know how to read a lead sheet. Together; these attributes help the player to create the appropriate musical part on their instrument: Ex 1.1 Straight I6th Funk _ 102 Intro Bb7(9) Drums Only Drums Onlv IS Es79) 2 Drumset Interpretation Lead sheets do not contain "beats" The drummer must create the appropriate feel (and corresponding sound) based on the style indicated (ballad, 2-feel; Latin, shuffle; etc:) Since a lead sheet is not a drum chart; it is neither necessary nor desirable for the drummer to play every figure (or rhythm) that is written within the music You must be much more selective when choosing which figures to play: Plaving time _through the majority of the figures and marking _the form should be thenorm: Fills and fill markers are not written in either: As a result; it is also up to the drummer to decide when (and if) a transition fill is necessary: Therefore, your own personal interpretation and creativity are vital in shaping the music Lastly, it is important to note that you must still observe the principles that we have discussed in the last 3 Drum Performance books: time, feel, note durations, and marking the form: Drum Performance 4 Bb7(9)

Unit Chapter 2 Soul-Jazz Sound As stated earlier; this chart (although a funk tune) is coming from the jazz tradition and perspective Therefore, your drumset approach and sound should come from this angle. Please use the following articulations: 1 Open "Non-Buried" Bass Drum: This will mimic the small legato bass drums used in jazz If you bury the beater; you will sound more like contemporary fusion than soul (or jazz) . 2. Snare Drum "Light Rim Shot": You can use rim shots within this tune; but they should not be "rock" rim shots They should be at a lower dynamic, used to get the drum to "speak"_~rather than just for volume: In addition, feel free to experiment with playing the center of the head or off- center (no rim shot) This will help you to sound more authentic like funk-jazz greats Idris Muhammad; Bill Stewart; Jabo Starks and Clyde Stubblefield: 3. Loose Hi-Hat: The hi-hat should not be too clean and tight in this style Try to make it a tad loose and dirty like older New Orleans jazz, soul and funk recordings If it is too clean you will sound more pop-fusion than jazz

Background: Lead Sheet Interpretation 2 As we learned in Unit 1, a lead sheet is a modest; sketch chart that only specifies a song's melody; chord structure; form and some basic information about the style (for example; "medium swing"). However; in this unit's lesson we will combine this new information with more traditional concepts from AABA and ABAB forms (Drum Performance books 1 and 2). Thus, each letter will now receive a different rhythm, a specific phrase-length and a particular melody and chord structure There will also be two structure additions: an open vamp at the beginning of the chart and a coda: Each of these items is now present in the lead sheet: For example: Ex 2.1 165 Swung Sths Second Line Feel Vamp Till Cue Open vamp 8 AZ E7 A7 Letter A: On cue, four bars repeated: B Bb7 Eb Letter B: three bars in 5/4,one bar in 7/4. Unit 2 - Chapter 2 New Orleans Contemporary Jazz Sound As stated earlier; this chart; although a contemporary tune; is coming from the jazz tradition and perspective Therefore; your drumset approach should reflect this perspective. 1. Open "Non-Buried" Bass Drum: This will mimic the small legato bass drums used in jazz and traditional New Orleans second line rhythms If you bury the beater; you will create a sound closer to contemporary fusion than to jazz (or New Orleans second line): 2. Snare Drum "Light Rim Shot": You can use rim shots within this tune; but they should not be "rock"rim shots They should be ata lower dynamic; used to get the drum to "speak"__rather than just for volume: In addition; feel free to experiment with playing the center of the head or off- center (no rim shot) This will helpyou to sound more authentic; like jazz greats Idris Muhammad, Bill Stewart; Stanton Moore and Johnny Vidacovich: 3. The Ride Cymbal: In this style; the ride cymbal should not be too staccato or tight: It should follow the concepts discussed in the previous quarter's jazz charts, utilizing a long; open tone; with all accents being "shouldered" Refer to Drum Performance 3 ifyou need to review this topic 21

Chapter 3: Basic Independence The study of coordinated independence is essential in order to gain command and control of the drumset: The method presented in this (and upcoming units) introduces you to a "tried and true" method to begin developing the control needed to perform at a high level on your instrument To acquire these skills, you must develop disciplined and meticulous practice method There is no substitute for practicing slowly and in a controlled manner In these exercises, the ride cymbal and hi-hat will play a continuous pattern while the snare drum and bass drum are placed in various downbeat and upbeat groupings The cymbal pattern is a common pattern used in all genres of music which is a quarter note on beats 1 and 3 and two eighth notes on beats 2 and 4. In addition, the hi-hat is played with the foot on beats 2 and 4 as follows: Ex 1.9 Ride cymbal Hi-hat with foot When played as straight eighth notes this pattern can be used in Latin, funk; rock, R&B, metal, etc: This common pattern can also be interpreted as swung eighth notes for swung styles such as jazz and hip-hop: As presented in Drum Performance 3, swung eighth notes may be indicated at the top of a chart with a symbol indicating all written eighth notes are swung unless otherwise indicated. For example: Ex 1.10 J-r;_ S Swung eighth notes indicator Equals General Rules There are a few general rules to keep in mind when beginning independence exercises 1 . When the snare drum (or bass drum) lands on a downbeat; it will always be paired with the ride cymbal. Ride cymbal and snare drum together on every downbeat Ex 1.11 Drum Technique 3

Chapter 4: Independence Exercises These exercises should be played in the following methods: 1_ Swung eighth notes with the jazz ride cymbal pattern, 90-105-120 BPM 2 Straight eighth notes, 70-80-90 BPM: Ex 1.15 Isolated downbeats 10 Drum Technique 3

Chapter 2: Accented Eighth-Note Triplets Drum Technique 2 introduced accented 16th notes and eighth-note triplets The following exercises build on your knowledge and practice Certain popular music styles (mainly those in more of a jazz idiom) require you to be able to phrase in a swing manner These exercises are designed to reinforce the swing feel This is also an opportunity to practice the up motion, which has been instructed throughout the Technique series: The note before each accented note can be articulated asan up motion. Single-handed practice (especially when concentrating on the weak hand) will improve your technique; speed and endurance It is imperative that your articulation of all subdivisions is clean and precise, and that all notes (snare drum; bass drum and hi-hat) all line up with no flamming between sound surfaces Presented is a simple yet classic bass drum and hi-hat ostinato. Do not accent the bass drum when you accent the snare drum: Metronome marking for these exercises: BPM =60...80...104 These exercises should be played in four ways: 1 . Right hand lead: R-L-R-L 2. Left hand lead: L-R-L-R 3. Exclusively with the right hand: R-R-R-R 4 Exclusively with the left hand: L-L-L-L Ex 2.10 3 E; 5 16 Drum Technique 3

Chapter 3: Coordinated Independence This further study of coordination and independence focuses on upbeat eighth notes Care must be taken, as with all exercises, with proper execution of subdivisions Review Unit Chapter 3 for the methods for ensuring that these exercises are performed correctly: Strive at all times for a consistent sound from all parts of the drumset: Practicing with a click is essential: These exercises should be played in the following methods: 1_ Swung eighth notes with the jazz ride cymbal pattern, 90 _ 105 -120 BPM: 2 Straight eighth notes, 70 80 90 BPM: Isolated upbeats Ex 2.11 18 Drum Technique 3

Unit 4 - Chapter 3 Chapter 3: Coordinated Independence Exercises Utilizing the same ride cymbal pattern from previous units, introduced now are rhythmic patterns played between snare drum, bass drum and hi-hat: These exercises will enhance your command and control of the drumset by accentuating the intercommunication between all the limbs Your complete attention to detail is required in order to perform all levels ofthese exercises Sound balance and articulation are essential in performing these patterns correctly: The first set of exercises will be performed using a jazz phrasing; where the written even eighth notes are interpreted as swung eighths: Exercises 4.18a, b, € and d are all written out for you: For Exercise 4.18a you are playing the jazzride cymbal; with hi-hat on beats 2 and 4. You will play the written phrase between snare drum and bass drum: Exercise 4.18b has you performing the jazz ride cymbal pattern: You will play the written phrase between snare drum and hi-hat; Exercise 4.18c has you performing the written phrase between snare drum and bass drum with the hi-hat on beats 2 and 4 Exercise 4.18d has you performing the written phrase between snare drum and hi hat: All exercises will be performed in this manner; i.e. with four distinct steps Ex 4.19 Ex 4.20 33

Chapter 2: Jazz Style Eighth-Note Triplet Study Continuing your study of coordinated independence; we will now venture into eighth-note triplet phrasing between all limbs. As with our study with eighth-note and 16th-note phrases, you will be performing these exercises using four levels, which are written for you. The fifth and very important level is your contribution as a creative individual This fifth level will be examined in your final exam, so it is most important that you develop a high skill of notation, as this will be graded as well. Your precise articulation of the eighth-note triplets is essential for these exercises This concentration on articulation will help every level of your drumset performance: It will also help immensely with your jazz phrasing: Ex 7.2 EE 7 7 FF4 Ex 7.3 1 TFD 7565 48 Drum Technique 3

Chapter 2: Coordinated Independence A further examination of eighth-note triplets opposite a jazz ride pattern will have you learning very typical phrases, applicable in many musical contexts. Very importantly, the hi-hat remains on beats 2 and 4 Execution needs to be exacting; with triplets phrased perfectly: Keeping the tempos slow will ensure that your practice time is invested well: BPM = 60...72...80 Ex 10.7 Ex 10.8 1 R LR 76 R L R R L R R L R R L R R L R Doubling the bass drum note provides an exciting edge to this application: Ex 10.9 H R L R R L R R L R R L R R L R R L R Rephrasing the eighth-note triplets as 16th notes andthe 16th notes as 32nd notesyields this practical phrase: Ex 10.10 Ex 10.11 Ex 10.12 T) E R L R R L R R L R F R LR R LR R L R 66 Drum Technique 3

Unit 1 Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Over-the-Bar Groove Technique As you have learned from the Drum Performance series, drummer must be able to interact and communicate (musically and visually) with the rest of an ensemble. While technique does not necessarily tellyou what to play (how to interact appropriately with developed musical ideas), technique does provide you with the facility to be creative and play what comes to your mind in an instant: Common genres that allow for expansive interaction and creativity are jazz, funk, gospel, Latin and jam bands (Phish, John Scofield, String Cheese Incident and Widespread Panic) While a drummer must maintain steady groove, they must also be able to interact without harming the integrity of the groove Over-the-Bar Patterns To play"over the bar" means to play a repeated musical phrase (usually grouped in an odd amount of beats) against the grain of a groove template. Playing small groupings of ideas that do not land on beat 1 of a bar is a great technique to help you develop creative freedom: The dotted eighth note is a very useful rhythm to play in 4/4 that will help you begin to feel over-the-bar concepts. Play quarter notes with both feet while your hands play unison dotted eighth notes (or tied eighths and 16ths) between the floor tom and snare drum: The dotted eighth note is worth one and a half beats (or three I6th notes) Therefore; when repeated this pattern creates a three beat phrase which lands on downbeat every three beats. To start; we will use a two bar phrase as follows: Ex 1.9 Three beats Three beats Hands Feet In the above example; the three-beat phrase repeats on beat 4 of the first measure, creating an over-the- bar phrase To take this concept a step further; lets apply the same three-beat phrase to a four-bar phrase; as follows: Ex 1.10 Hands Feet

Unit 5 - Chapter 2 Unit Six reading | techNIque perfOrMance Chapter 1: Closed Triplet Rolls In Unit 3,closed rolls were presented as a way to develop your touch and finger  control In this unit, you will apply the same closed roll concepts to triplet-based rolls and rhythms This technique will help you to become expressive when playing jazz and other triplet-based styles: To developa smooth triplet-based roll start by playing alternating (hand-to-hand) eighth note triplets at 110 BPM Do not accent any notes and play in a relaxed, yet controlled manner; as follows: Ex 6.1 Next, double (or "diddle") every single stroke to produce 16th-note triplets, as follows: Ex 6.2 3 RR LL RR RR LL RR LL RR LL LC RR *written out fully: Ex 6.3 3 = R   R L L   R R L L   R   R L L  R   R L L  R   R L L  R   R L Next; alternate one measure of single strokes and one measure of doubles, as follows: Ex 6.4 3 3 )F R L R L   R L   R L  R L   R L RR LL RR LL RR LL RR LL RR LL RR LL As stated in Unit 3, it very important factor is to maintain your single stoke "primary pulse" when playing closed rolls. By doing this,you will realize that the finger - pressure is what produces the"buzz" Now; play each stroke as a closed (buzz) roll At 110 BPM, you may notice a slight break between each stroke: Again, adjust the pressure within the hands to shorten or elongate the length of each multiple rebound. Ex 6.5 3 3 3 7 R ~  L _ + R _ [~ R _ + L_ > R -  L_ + R3+ [~+ R _ + L_ # 54 Drum Technique 4

Chapter 2: Drum Soloing Concepts Drum solos are a chance for the drummer to shine; be creative and add excitement to a piece of music: When taking a drum solo it is very easy to get overwhelmed with ideas, therefore you must think ahead and have a seed of an idea to start your solo. You can then develop your idea in similar fashion to the concepts presented in this book (compression/expansion; over-the-bar ideas, etc ). One way to build your own repertoire of"go to"ideas is to research past drummers""go to"ideas. Drummers such as Philly Joe Jones, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams, John Bonham, Neil Peart and Steve Jordan have provided the drumming community with classic concepts that are essential to the development of great drum soloing: Drum solos can be categorized as follows: The "open" solo: An extended, unaccompanied drum solo that typically changes tempos and has rubato (free-form, out-of-time) sections Soloing over the form: Common in the jazz idiom, this style of solo follows the form (AABA, ABAB, Blues; etc) of the tune just as a melodic instrumentalist would: Soloing over a rhythmic vamp: A drum solo over a set of ensemble figures, usually in a two or four-bar pattern: Groove-based solo: This drum solo is primarily a groove with variations and fill ideas either unaccompanied or over a bass line: Examples of Each Solo Style Open Solos Open solos were very popular in the classic rock era of the 1970s and 1980s. Drummers such as John Bonham; lan Paice; Carl Palmer and Neil Peart are commonly associated with this style of soloing: Below are few brief highlights from this era. John Bonham uses nice phrasing in this 32nd-note lick featuring doubles on a single bass drum, as follows: Ex 10.10 Bonham then plays a triplet-based idea that was originally played by Elvin Jones For example: Ex 10.11 F E2 Neil Peart also pays homage to the early jazz masters by "borrowing" this idea from Art Blakey: Note: Billy Cobham got a lot of mileage out ofthis idea as well: Ex 10.12 88 Drum Technique 4

Unit 10 - Chapter 2 Soloing over the Form Soling over the form of a tune is a staple for jazz music. In this concept; the drummer must be aware of the form at all times Many drummers in the jazz era such as Art Blakey; Max Roach and Elvin Jones would use the melody of the tune for rhythmic ideas, masterfully incorporating them into their solos. Philly Joe Jones would use a variety of approaches when trading fours with the other ensemble members For example; these are all ideas from just a few choruses of the same tune: Ex 10.13 3 3 7 3 3 3 3 4 Soloing over a Rhythmic Vamp Soloing over vamp was made popular by jazz crossover guitarists such as John Scofield and Alan Holdsworth: Drummers like Dennis Chambers, Steve Gadd, Tony Williams and Steve Jordan have recorded timeless solos over vamps These solos are especially challenging due to the fact that ensemble figures are present: A drummer must play the figures while soloing in between, around and over the figures: Steve Gadd provides solid ideas that work in all genres. For example; here are a few ideas he uses to set up the"&"of beat 4 Ex 10.14 7 L L   R L R L L   R L   R L L   R L   R

About the Authors Stewart Jean was born and raised in Deptford, New Jersey, where he was introduced to music through his father's love of classical music and his friends' Led Zeppelin LPs. Stewart's passion for music grew as he studied drumset; timpani and mallets in high school. Having attained many awards in state competitions, Stewart decided to set his goals on becoming a professional musician: lnuug At age eighteen, Stewart enrolled in the Studio Music and Jazz Program at the University of Miami where he honed his performance and writing skills Upon graduating with a Bachelor of Music degree; Stewart became a sought-after freelance musician in the Miami area performing on many recordings, tours and shows. After many successful years in Miami; Stewart relocated to Los Angeles; maintaining steady workflow: Stewart has performed or recorded behind many prominent artists such as Jimmy Buffett; Raul Midon, Preston Smith and Bruce Kulick Stewart's direct and to-the- point drumming has kept him at the top of many artists' lists. Stewart is currently the Program Chair for the Drum Program at Musicians Institute in Hollywood; California: Having received expert mentoring throughout his years, Stewart has developed his educational offerings towards the goal of providing clear and positive nurturing to young students Stewart has created diverse curricula for the school such as Analysis of Great Grooves, Drum Set Soloing and Introduction to Keyboards. 92 Drum Technique 4

Since moving to LA. in 2003, Kevin Stevens has played with the house band (Cleto and the Cletones) for "Jimmy Kimmel Live" performed with Shakira on The Ellen DeGeneres Show" toured the globe with the likes of Chilean pop superstar Beto Cuevas (La Ley), the critically acclaimed Neon Neon, and actress/singer Minnie Driver Kevin was a member of the original cast for the hit Broadway musical/movie Rock   of   Ages and is also the leader/founder of the internationally renowned funk/jazz combo Brass Monkey Brass Band. Kevin is graduate of the acclaimed University of Miami jazz department with a BA in Studio Music and Jazz Performance. Since arriving at Musicians Institute in 2009, Kevin has been known as one of the instructors to go to in order to get your groove together: Kevin teaches Private Lessons, Drumset Performance, Music Minus One,   along with New Orleans Drumming a class which he created for Ml which covers the history ofthe drum set and the role of the instrument in jazz, R&B, rock & roll, funk and beyondl Kevin is proud to endorse these fine companies: Sabian Cymbals, Vic Firth Drumsticks, Remo Drumheads and Protection Racket Cases: 94 Drum Technique 4

CD Track 5 5. Cmon Now To match the style and tempo of this song, lighten up the feel and keep the time "floating' over the top of the tune. Comp less and let the time breathe more_ Listen to Tony Williams on some of the classic uptempo Miles Davis tracks, and notice how his time breathes and how he lets his ride cymbal carry the time_ Motion is a key ingredient to helping the feel_ If you consciously lighten up your cymbal motion and think of a legato flowing stroke when playing figures (i.e: the intro), it will reflect in your time. Also, try to be aware of letting the stick do more and more of the work when playing faster tempos. Practice the introduction figure over and over until it is very comfortable Use variety of fills to work in and out of the rhythms. Play this song in very relaxed and comfortable manner and if you have to think about playing the song's figures, they will sound stiff . During the intro, all of the figures are long and legato, so they should be phrased long with "shouldered" cym- bal, rather than short on a snare drum or bass drum only: When you crash your ride cymbal, try to drop the shoul- der of the stick against the bOw of the cymbal; don't crash it on the edge like a normal crash: Listen to the soloists during this tune and notice how their lines ascend and descend: Usually, when ascends, you should increase in volume, and when it descends, you should decrease in volume_ line During the trading-fours section of the song, phrase the last four-bar section to make it obvious that this is the end of the form, and that you are ready to g0 back to the top of the form and play the head out. A natural way to end this tune would be to hit the last note with light cymbal crash, then swell the volume, then decrescendo and end with a light crash and bass drum note_ Be sure to let that last note ring out, to match the phrasing of the tune IRETSCH Jazz great Tony Williams

Angel Performance Notes This is "acoustic drum and bass jazz-fusion with a hint of latin flair" or something along those lines. The song starts with some programmed sounds and loops: The live drums start with a "non-descript groove;' meaning that / am playing time but / am trying not to play a repetitive cycle. No "beat" per se; it'$ more of a very open; improvised and constantly changing rhythmic structure: play a half-open hi-hat to create a more washy sound and play no rim-shots on the snare at all, just fairly quiet mid-head strokes, buzz rolls, etc: After 16 bars of this evolving intro, | change the indifferent feel to a very clear beat: | play something resembling a drum-and-bass groove with rim-shots, tight hi-hats and lots of ghost notes. The snare hits on the 2 and 3 of every Ist and 3rd bar of the 4-bar pattern to clearly define the cycle but at the same time there are lots of variations and subtle changes in the rhythm that give the groove a Ioose and jazzy feel: tried to play a "human version" of a drum and bass feel: The right hand part is quite tricky to play because the fast groups of three I6th-notes require finger control technique, whereas the more powerful straight 8th-notes need to be played using locked grip (wrist control strokes) , so you have to employ different playing techniques in quick succession; always changing back and forth between finger-control and wrist control. There are a few subtle unison accents and phrases in the song but / tried to make those sound more like part of the groove rather than deliberate accents, like the quarter-note triplet pick-up of the verse melody for example. There are also a lot of "starts and stops" in the groove; usually accentuating key notes of the melody: This also is a cliche of jungle or drum and bass music and try to incorporate this idea quite a bit / also change sounds strategically when a new section begins. / switch from one hi-hat to another and alter the pattern slightly to recreate a "programmed feel" and to create dynamic and timbral contrast between all the sections of the song: The climax of the tune is the bridge in the middle where [ am playing the ride cymbal and hitting accents along with the guitar and vocal melody: After that | bring the volume down again to "cruise out:" The drum part ends with an improvised "groove solo: This signals that we 've come full circle: The song starts with this open, evolving feel; a loose, improvised "vibe_ Then it develops, the groove goes through different stages of dynamic levels and orchestration and then returns to this open; loose, improvised outro that is reminiscent of the intro feel: NOTATION KEY open hi-hat China or crash jingle hats X-hat ride bass drums toms hi-hat wl foot snare
